The **Union Jack**[[1]](#cite_note-Flag_Institute_terminology-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) or **Union Flag** is the [national flag](/wiki/National_flag "National flag") of the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom").

The flag is an overlay of the [cross of Saint George](/wiki/St_George%27s_Cross "St George's Cross") (the [patron saint](/wiki/Patron_saint "Patron saint") of [England](/wiki/England "England")), edged in white, [superimposed](/wiki/Superimposition "Superimposition") on the red [saltire of Saint Patrick](/wiki/Saint_Patrick%27s_Saltire "Saint Patrick's Saltire") (the patron saint of [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland "Ireland")), also edged in white, superimposed on the [saltire of Saint Andrew](/wiki/Flag_of_Scotland "Flag of Scotland") (the patron saint of [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland")).[[3]](#cite_note-GarvinHooperCox1929-3) [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales") is not represented in the flag because the earlier [flag of Great Britain](/wiki/Flag_of_Great_Britain "Flag of Great Britain") was designed while Wales was part of the [Kingdom of England](/wiki/Kingdom_of_England "Kingdom of England").

The origins of the flag date to the earlier flag of Great Britain which was established in 1606 by a [proclamation](/wiki/Proclamation "Proclamation") of King [James VI and I](/wiki/James_VI_and_I "James VI and I") of [Scotland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Scotland "Kingdom of Scotland") and [England](/wiki/Kingdom_of_England "Kingdom of England").[[4]](#cite_note-4) The present design was established by an [order in council](/wiki/Order_in_council "Order in council") following the [Act of Union 1800](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1800 "Acts of Union 1800"), which joined the [Kingdom of Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain") and the [Kingdom of Ireland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Ireland "Kingdom of Ireland") to create the [United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland"). It was unchanged following the secession of the [Irish Free State](/wiki/Irish_Free_State "Irish Free State") in 1922.

It is sometimes asserted that the term *Union Jack* properly refers only to naval usage, but this assertion was dismissed by the [Flag Institute](/wiki/Flag_Institute "Flag Institute") in 2013 after historical investigations.[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-Marshall_2017_p.110-111-7)[[a]](#cite_note-9)

The flags of [British Overseas Territories](/wiki/British_Overseas_Territories "British Overseas Territories"), as well as certain sovereign states and regions (particularly in [the Commonwealth](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations")) that were previously [British possessions](/wiki/British_possessions "British possessions"), incorporate the Union Jack into their own flag designs or have official flags that are derived from the Union Jack. Many of [these flags](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_territories_with_the_Union_Jack_displayed_on_their_flag "List of countries and territories with the Union Jack displayed on their flag") are [blue](/wiki/Blue_ensign "Blue ensign") or [red ensigns](/wiki/Red_ensign "Red ensign") with the Union Jack in the [canton](/wiki/Canton_(flag) "Canton (flag)") and [defaced](/wiki/Defacement_(flag) "Defacement (flag)") with the distinguishing arms of the territory. The governors of British Overseas Territories and the [Australian states](/wiki/Australian_states "Australian states") also have personal standards that incorporate the Union Jack in their design. The flag continues to have official status in [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), by parliamentary resolution, where it is also known as the **Royal Union Flag**.[[9]](#cite_note-ruf-10)

## Terminology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Union_Jack&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Terminology")]

The terms *Union Jack* and *Union Flag* are both used historically for describing the national flag of the United Kingdom.

Evolution of the Union Flag

Initially, the flag was termed the 'British flag' or 'flag of Britain', and the term 'Union' first appeared in 1625.[[1]](#cite_note-Flag_Institute_terminology-1) The etymology of *jack* in the context of flagstaffs reaches back to Middle German. The suffix *-kin* was used in Middle Dutch and Middle German as a [diminutive](/wiki/Diminutive "Diminutive").[[10]](#cite_note-OED-kin-11) Examples occur in both [Chaucer](/wiki/Chaucer "Chaucer") and [Langland](/wiki/William_Langland "William Langland") though the form is unknown in Old English.[[10]](#cite_note-OED-kin-11) *John* is a common male forename (going back to the Bible), appearing in Dutch as *Jan*. Both languages use it as a generic form for a man in general.[[11]](#cite_note-OEDJohn-12) The two were combined in the Middle Dutch *Janke*, whence Middle French *Jakke* and Middle English *Jack*.[[12]](#cite_note-OEDJackn2-13) *Jack* came to be used to identify all manner of particularly small objects or small versions of larger ones. The *[Oxford English Dictionary](/wiki/Oxford_English_Dictionary "Oxford English Dictionary")* has definition III.21 "Something insignificant, or smaller than the normal size" and gives examples from 1530 to 2014 of this usage.[[12]](#cite_note-OEDJackn2-13) Further examples in the compounds section at 2b illustrate this.[[12]](#cite_note-OEDJackn2-13) The original maritime flag use of *jack* was "A ship's flag of a smaller size than the ensign, used at sea as a signal, or as an identifying device".[[13]](#cite_note-OEDjackn4-14) The jack was flown in the bows or from the head of the spritsail mast to indicate the vessel's nationality: "You are alsoe for this present service to keepe in yor Jack at yor Boultspritt end and yor Pendant and yor Ordinance"[[14]](#cite_note-15) The Union Flag when instantiated as a small jack became known as the "Union Jack" and this later term transferred to more general usage of the Union Flag.[[15]](#cite_note-16)

Also later a short flagpole was placed in the [bows of a ship](/wiki/Bow_(watercraft) "Bow (watercraft)") to fly the jack, this became known as the *[jackstaff](/wiki/Jackstaff "Jackstaff")*.[[16]](#cite_note-OEDjackstaff-17)

According to the [Flag Institute](/wiki/Flag_Institute "Flag Institute"), a membership-run [vexillological](/wiki/Vexillological "Vexillological") charity,[[17]](#cite_note-18) "the national flag of the United Kingdom, the Crown Dependencies and Overseas Territories is the Union Flag, which may also be called the Union Jack."[[18]](#cite_note-british-flag-protocol-19) The institute has also stated:

> it is often stated that the Union Flag should only be described as the Union Jack when flown in the bows of a warship, but this is a relatively recent idea. From early in its life the Admiralty itself frequently referred to the flag as the Union Jack, whatever its use, and in 1902 an Admiralty circular announced that Their Lordships had decided that either name could be used officially. In 1908, a government minister stated, in response to a parliamentary question, that "the Union Jack should be regarded as the National flag".[[19]](#cite_note-FlagInstitute-20)[[20]](#cite_note-21)

Notwithstanding Their Lordships' circular of 1902, by 1913 the Admiralty described the "Union Flag" and added in a footnote that "A Jack is a Flag to be flown only on the 'Jack' Staff".[[21]](#cite_note-22)

However, the authoritative *[A Complete Guide to Heraldry](/wiki/A_Complete_Guide_to_Heraldry "A Complete Guide to Heraldry")* published in 1909 by [Arthur Charles Fox-Davies](/wiki/Arthur_Charles_Fox-Davies "Arthur Charles Fox-Davies") uses the term "Union Jack".[[22]](#cite_note-23)

The term "Union Flag" is used in [King Charles I's](/wiki/Charles_I_of_England "Charles I of England") 1634 proclamation:

> ... none of Our Subjects, of any of Our Nations and Kingdoms shall from henceforth presume to carry the Union Flag in the Main top, or other part of any of their Ships (that is) St Georges cross and St Andrew's Cross joined together upon pain of Our high displeasure, but that the same Union Flag be still reserved as an ornament proper for Our own Ships and Ships in our immediate Service and Pay, and none other.[[23]](#cite_note-Perrin_59-24)

— Proclamation appointing the Flag, as well for Our Navy Royal as for the Ships of Our Subjects of South and North Britain – 5 May 1634

and in [King George III](/wiki/George_III_of_the_United_Kingdom "George III of the United Kingdom")'s proclamation of 1 January 1801 concerning the arms and flag of the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland:

> And that the Union Flag shall be Azure, the Crosses Saltires of St. Andrew and St. Patrick Quarterly per Saltire, counterchanged Argent and Gules; the latter fimbriated of the Second, surmounted by the Cross of St. George of the Third, fimbriated as the Saltire : ...[[24]](#cite_note-25)

— A Proclamation Declaring His Majesty's Pleasure concerning the Royal Style and Titles appertaining to the Imperial Crown of the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland, and its Dependencies, and also the Ensigns, Armorial Flags, and Banners thereof

When the first flag representing Britain was introduced on the [proclamation](/wiki/Proclamation "Proclamation") of [King James I](/wiki/James_VI_and_I "James VI and I") in 1606,[[25]](#cite_note-26) it became known simply as the "British flag" or the "flag of Britain". The royal proclamation gave no distinctive name to the new flag. At the [funeral of King James](/wiki/Death_and_funeral_of_James_VI_and_I "Death and funeral of James VI and I") in 1625 the flag was called the "Banner of the Union of the two Crosses of England and Scotland".[[26]](#cite_note-27) The word *jack* was in use before 1600 to describe the [maritime bow flag](/wiki/Jack_(flag) "Jack (flag)").[[19]](#cite_note-FlagInstitute-20) By 1627 a small Union Jack was commonly flown in this position. One theory goes that for some years it would have been called simply the "Jack", or "Jack flag", or the "King's Jack", but by 1674, while formally referred to as "His Majesty's Jack", it was commonly called the "Union Jack", and this was officially acknowledged.[[27]](#cite_note-flaginst-28)[[7]](#cite_note-Marshall_2017_p.110-111-7)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/15/The_True_Portraicture_of_His_Maties_Royall_Ship_the_Soveraigne_of_the_Seas._Built_in_the_Yeare_1637_%28detail_of_the_Union_flag_on_the_jackstaff%29.jpg/250px-The_True_Portraicture_of_His_Maties_Royall_Ship_the_Soveraigne_of_the_Seas._Built_in_the_Yeare_1637_%28detail_of_the_Union_flag_on_the_jackstaff%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_True_Portraicture_of_His_Maties_Royall_Ship_the_Soveraigne_of_the_Seas._Built_in_the_Yeare_1637_(detail_of_the_Union_flag_on_the_jackstaff).jpg)

A portrait of a ship, with the Union Jack placed on a jackstaff at the ship's bow, 1637

A proclamation issued by King [George III](/wiki/George_III "George III") at the time of the [Union of 1801](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1800 "Acts of Union 1800") concerned flags at sea and repeatedly referred to "Ensigns, Flags, Jacks, and Pendants" and forbade merchant vessels from wearing "Our Jack, commonly called the Union Jack" nor any pendants or colours used by the King's ships.[[28]](#cite_note-London_Gazette_1801-01-03-29) Reinforcing the distinction the King's proclamation of the same day concerning the arms and flag of the United Kingdom (not colours at sea) called the new flag "the Union Flag".[[29]](#cite_note-30)

The size and power of the [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy") internationally at the time could also explain why the flag was named the "Union Jack"; considering the navy was so widely utilised and renowned by the United Kingdom and [colonies](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire"), it is possible that the term *jack* occurred because of its regular use on all British ships using the jackstaff (a flag pole attached to the bow of a ship). The name may alternatively come from the 'jack-et' of the English or Scottish [soldiers](/wiki/Soldier "Soldier"), or from the name of James I who originated the first union in 1603. Even if the term *Union Jack* does derive from the jack flag, after three centuries, it is now sanctioned by use and has appeared in official use, confirmed as the national flag by Parliament and remains the popular term.[[30]](#cite_note-Flagsofworld-31)

[Winston Churchill](/wiki/Winston_Churchill "Winston Churchill"), [British Prime Minister](/wiki/British_Prime_Minister "British Prime Minister") from 1940 to 1945, referred to the flag of the United Kingdom as the Union Jack. In March 1899, Churchill wrote to [his mother](/wiki/Lady_Randolph_Churchill "Lady Randolph Churchill") from [India](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj") about her plans to produce a new trans-Atlantic magazine, to be called *The Anglo-Saxon Review*. The drawing at the end of this letter was deliberately facetious, teasing her for going down-market, and in the accompanying letter he wrote, "Your title 'The Anglo Saxon' with its motto 'Blood is thicker than water' only needs the Union Jack & the [Star Spangled Banner](/wiki/Flag_of_the_United_States "Flag of the United States") crossed on the cover to be suited to one of [Harmsworth](/wiki/Alfred_Harmsworth,_1st_Viscount_Northcliffe "Alfred Harmsworth, 1st Viscount Northcliffe")'s cheap Imperialist productions."[[31]](#cite_note-32)

More recently, Reed's *Nautical Almanac* (1990 edition) unambiguously stated: "The Union Flag, frequently but incorrectly referred to as the Union Jack, ..." and later: "8. The Jack – A small flag worn on a jackstaff on the stem of Naval Vessels. The Royal Navy wears the Union Flag ... This is the only occasion when it correct to describe the flag as the Union Jack".[[32]](#cite_note-33) However, this assertion does not appear in any Reed's *Nautical Almanac* since 1993. In the 2016 Reed's *Nautical Almanac*, the only entry where this might appear, section 5.21, covering Flag Etiquette, does not include this statement. Within the *Almanac*, neither the Union Flag nor the Union Jack are included pictorially or mentioned by name.

For comparison with another anglophone country with a large navy, *[jack of the United States](/wiki/Jack_of_the_United_States "Jack of the United States")* specifically refers to the flag flown from the jackstaff of a warship, auxiliary or other U.S. governmental entity.[[33]](#cite_note-34)

The *Butcher's Apron* is a [pejorative](/wiki/Pejorative "Pejorative") term for the flag, common amongst [Irish republicans](/wiki/Irish_republicanism "Irish republicanism"), citing the blood-streaked appearance of the flag and referring to atrocities committed in Ireland and other countries under British colonial rule.[[34]](#cite_note-35)[[35]](#cite_note-36) In 2006, [Sandra White](/wiki/Sandra_White "Sandra White"), a [Member of the Scottish Parliament](/wiki/Member_of_the_Scottish_Parliament "Member of the Scottish Parliament"), caused a furore when the term was used in a press release under her name. It was later blamed on the actions of a researcher, who resigned yet claimed that the comment had been approved by White.[[36]](#cite_note-apron-37) The Irish folk band [the Wolfe Tones](/wiki/The_Wolfe_Tones "The Wolfe Tones") has a song entitled "The Butcher's Apron" which makes reference to the term.[[37]](#cite_note-38)

In the [Chinese language](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"), the flag has the nickname *Rice-Character Flag* (米字旗; [Mandarin](/wiki/Mandarin_Chinese "Mandarin Chinese") [Pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *mǐzìqí*, [Cantonese](/wiki/Cantonese "Cantonese") [Jyutping](/wiki/Jyutping "Jyutping"): *mai5zi6kei4*) because the pattern looks similar to the Chinese character for "rice" (米).[[38]](#cite_note-39)

## Design

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Union_Jack&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Design")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/UnionJack_Construction_Per_1801_Blazon.svg/250px-UnionJack_Construction_Per_1801_Blazon.svg.png)](/wiki/File:UnionJack_Construction_Per_1801_Blazon.svg)

Construction diagram of the Union Jack, as described in the 1801 blazon

The current flag's design has been in use since 1801. Its original [blazon](/wiki/Blazon "Blazon"), as decreed by [George III of the United Kingdom](/wiki/George_III_of_the_United_Kingdom "George III of the United Kingdom") on 1 January 1801, reads:

> the Union flag shall be azure, the crosses-saltires of St. Andrew and St. Patrick quartered per saltire counter changed argent and gules; the latter fimbriated of the second [viz., argent]; surmounted by the cross of St. George of the third [viz., gules], fimbriated as the saltire [viz., argent].[[39]](#cite_note-40)

### Specification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Union_Jack&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Specification")]

The Union Jack is normally twice as long as it is tall, a ratio of 1:2. In the United Kingdom, land flags are normally a ratio of 3:5; the Union Jack can also be made in this shape, but is 1:2 for most purposes. In 2008, MP [Andrew Rosindell](/wiki/Andrew_Rosindell "Andrew Rosindell") proposed a [Ten Minute Rule](/wiki/Ten_Minute_Rule "Ten Minute Rule") bill to standardise the design of the flag at 3:5, but the bill did not proceed past the first reading.[[40]](#cite_note-41)

The three-component crosses that make up the Union Jack are sized as follows:[[41]](#cite_note-Flag_Institute-42)

* The red [St George's Cross](/wiki/St_George%27s_Cross "St George's Cross") width is 1⁄5 of the flag's height with a 1⁄15 flag height [fimbriation](/wiki/Fimbriation "Fimbriation")
* The white diagonal [St Andrew's Cross](/wiki/Flag_of_Scotland "Flag of Scotland") width is 1⁄5 of the flag's height, visible on either side of the St Patrick's Cross in diagonals of 1⁄10 and 1⁄30 of the flag's height, respectively.
* The red diagonal [St Patrick's Cross](/wiki/Saint_Patrick%27s_Saltire "Saint Patrick's Saltire") width is 1⁄15 of the flag's height. It is offset by 1⁄30 of the flag's height in an anti-clockwise direction[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*]. According to the official blazon of 1801, the white diagonal [St Andrew's Cross](/wiki/Flag_of_Scotland "Flag of Scotland") is in fact [counterchanged](/wiki/Counterchanged "Counterchanged") with the red diagonal of [St Patrick's Cross](/wiki/Saint_Patrick%27s_Saltire "Saint Patrick's Saltire"). In this interpretation, the width of both saltires is 1⁄15 of the flag's height, with fimbriations of 1⁄30 of the flag's height on either side of the red saltire.

The crosses and fimbriations retain their thickness relative to the flag's *height* whether they are shown with a ratio of 3:5 or 1:2. *Height* here is the distance from top to bottom which in vexillology is termed [width or breadth](/wiki/Glossary_of_vexillology#width_or_breadth "Glossary of vexillology").

The Admiralty in 1864 settled all official flags at proportions of 1:2, but the relative widths of the crosses remained unspecified, with the above conventions becoming standardised in the 20th century.[[42]](#cite_note-43) In the 19th century, the Union Flag was defined by the same blazon but could vary in its geometrical proportions.[[43]](#cite_note-44)

Although the most common ratio is 1:2, other ratios exist. The Royal Navy's flag code book, BR20 *Flags of All Nations*, states that both 1:2 and 3:5 versions are official.[[44]](#cite_note-35variant-45) In the 3:5 version, the innermost points of the lower left and upper right diagonals of the St Patrick's Cross are cut off or truncated. The [Garter King of Arms](/wiki/Garter_King_of_Arms "Garter King of Arms") also suggests a ratio of 3:5 as the suitable proportion for a Union Flag flown on land.[[45]](#cite_note-auto-46) The [war flag](/wiki/War_flag "War flag") used by the [British Army](/wiki/British_Army "British Army") has the proportions 3:5.[[46]](#cite_note-47)

The King's Harbour Master's flag, similar to the Pilot Jack, is a 1:2 flag that contains a white-bordered Union Flag that is longer than 1:2. The jacks of ships flying variants of the [Blue Ensign](/wiki/Blue_Ensign "Blue Ensign") are square and have a square Union Flag in the canton.[[44]](#cite_note-35variant-45) The [King's Colours of Army regiments](/wiki/Regulation_Colours#British_Army "Regulation Colours") are 36 by 43 inches (910 mm × 1,090 mm); on them, the bars of the cross and saltire are of equal width, as are their respective narrow fimbriations.[[47]](#cite_note-48)

The flag does not have reflection symmetry due to the slight pinwheeling of the St Patrick's and St Andrew's crosses, technically the *[counterchange](/wiki/Counterchanged "Counterchanged") of saltires*. Thus, there is a correct side up. It is one of two national flags with [two-fold rotational symmetry](/wiki/Rotational_symmetry#Discrete_rotational_symmetry "Rotational symmetry"), [symmetry group](/wiki/Cyclic_group "Cyclic group") C2, the other being the [flag of Trinidad and Tobago](/wiki/Flag_of_Trinidad_and_Tobago "Flag of Trinidad and Tobago"). The original specification of the Union Flag in the royal proclamation of 1 January 1801 did not contain a drawn pattern or express which way the saltires should lie; they were simply "counterchanged" and the red saltire fimbriated. Nevertheless, a convention was soon established which accords most closely with the description. The flag was deliberately designed with the Irish saltire slightly depressed at the hoist end to reflect the earlier union with Scotland, giving as it were seniority to the Saint Andrew's cross.

When statically displayed, the hoist is on the observer's left. To fly the flag correctly, the white of St Andrew is *above* the red of St Patrick in the upper hoist canton (the quarter at the top nearest to the flag-pole). This is expressed by the phrases *wide white top* and *broadside up*. An upside-down flag must be *turned over* to be flown correctly; *rotating it* 180 degrees will still result in an upside-down flag.

The first drawn pattern for the flag was in a parallel proclamation on 1 January 1801, concerning civil naval ensigns, which drawing shows the [red ensign](/wiki/Red_ensign "Red ensign") (also to be used as a red jack by [privateers](/wiki/Privateer "Privateer")). As it appears in the *[London Gazette](/wiki/London_Gazette "London Gazette")*, the broad stripe is where expected for three of the four quarters, but the upper left quarter shows the broad stripe below.[[28]](#cite_note-London_Gazette_1801-01-03-29)

It is often stated that a flag upside down is a form of distress signal or even a deliberate insult. In the case of the Union Flag, the difference is subtle and is easily missed by the uninformed. It is often displayed upside down inadvertently—even on commercially-made hand waving flags.[[52]](#cite_note-53)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Union_Jack&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: History")]

In 1603, [James VI of Scotland](/wiki/James_VI_of_Scotland "James VI of Scotland") inherited the [Kingdom of England](/wiki/Kingdom_of_England "Kingdom of England") (and the newly created [client state](/wiki/Client_state "Client state"), the [Kingdom of Ireland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Ireland "Kingdom of Ireland")) as James I, thereby [uniting the crowns](/wiki/Union_of_the_Crowns "Union of the Crowns") in a [personal union](/wiki/Personal_union "Personal union"). With Wales annexed into the [Kingdom of England](/wiki/Kingdom_of_England "Kingdom of England") under the [Laws in Wales Acts 1535 and 1542](/wiki/Laws_in_Wales_Acts_1535_and_1542 "Laws in Wales Acts 1535 and 1542"), James now ruled over all of the island of [Great Britain](/wiki/Great_Britain "Great Britain"), which he frequently described as a unified kingdom (though the parliaments of the Kingdom of England and the [Kingdom of Scotland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Scotland "Kingdom of Scotland") did not actually unify until the [Kingdom of Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain") was formed in 1707).[[30]](#cite_note-Flagsofworld-31) In the wake of the 1603 personal union, several designs for a new flag were drawn up, juxtaposing the [Saint George's Cross](/wiki/Saint_George%27s_Cross "Saint George's Cross") and the [St Andrew's Saltire](/wiki/Flag_of_Scotland "Flag of Scotland"), but none were acceptable to James:[[53]](#cite_note-BBC_20060410-54)

Early (c. 1604) design proposals (manuscript in the [National Library of Scotland](/wiki/National_Library_of_Scotland "National Library of Scotland"))[[53]](#cite_note-BBC_20060410-54)
