Various other designs for a common flag were drawn up following the union of the two Crowns in 1603, but were rarely, if ever, used.[[55]](#cite_note-plantagenet-56) One version showed St George's Cross with St Andrew's Cross in the canton, and another version placed the two crosses side by side.

A painted wooden ceiling [boss](/wiki/Boss_(architecture) "Boss (architecture)") from [Linlithgow Palace](/wiki/Linlithgow_Palace "Linlithgow Palace"), dated to about 1617, depicts the Scottish royal unicorn holding a flag where a blue [Saltire](/wiki/Saltire "Saltire") surmounts the red cross of St. George.

### 1606–1801

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Union_Jack&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 1606–1801")]

Main article: [Flag of Great Britain](/wiki/Flag_of_Great_Britain "Flag of Great Britain")

[![Red cross with white border over a white saltire and dark blue background.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/Flag_of_Great_Britain_%281707%E2%80%931800%29.svg/250px-Flag_of_Great_Britain_%281707%E2%80%931800%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Flag_of_Great_Britain_(1707%E2%80%931800).svg)

The first Union Flag from 1606

On 12 April 1606, a new flag to represent the regal union between England and Scotland was specified in a royal decree, according to which the flag of England (a red cross on a white background, known as St George's Cross), and the flag of Scotland (a white saltire on a blue background, known as the saltire or St Andrew's Cross), would be joined together,[[27]](#cite_note-flaginst-28) forming the flag of Great Britain and first union flag:

> By the King: Whereas, some differences hath arisen between Our subjects of South and North Britaine travelling by Seas, about the bearing of their Flagges: For the avoiding of all contentions hereafter. We have, with the advice of our Council, ordered: That from henceforth all our Subjects of this Isle and Kingdome of Great Britaine, and all our members thereof, shall beare in their main-toppe the Red Crosse, commonly called St George's Crosse, and the White Crosse, commonly called St Andrew's Crosse, joyned together according to the forme made by our heralds, and sent by Us to our Admerall to be published to our Subjects: and in their fore-toppe our Subjects of South Britaine shall weare the Red Crosse onely as they were wont, and our Subjects of North Britaine in their fore-toppe the White Crosse onely as they were accustomed.[[56]](#cite_note-57)

This royal flag was, at first, to be used only at sea on civil and military ships of both England and Scotland, whereas land forces continued to use their respective national banners.[[57]](#cite_note-KnowBritain-58) Flying the national flag at the mainmast had signified the [Admiral of the Narrow Seas](/wiki/Admiral_of_the_Narrow_Seas "Admiral of the Narrow Seas") (the [English Channel](/wiki/English_Channel "English Channel")) and confusion arose. In 1634, [King Charles I](/wiki/Charles_I_of_England "Charles I of England") restricted its use to [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy") ships.[[58]](#cite_note-Blomfield_1895-59)[[23]](#cite_note-Perrin_59-24) After the [Acts of Union 1707](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1707 "Acts of Union 1707"), the flag gained a regularised status as "the ensign armorial of the [Kingdom of Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain")", the newly created state. It was then adopted by land forces as well, although the blue field used on land-based versions more closely resembled that of the blue of the flag of Scotland.

Various shades of blue have been used in the saltire over the years. The ground of the current Union Flag is a deep "[navy](/wiki/Navy_blue "Navy blue")" blue ([Pantone](/wiki/Pantone "Pantone") 280), which can be traced to the colour used for the [Blue Ensign](/wiki/Blue_Ensign "Blue Ensign") of the [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy")'s historic "Blue Squadron". (Dark shades of colour were used on [maritime flags](/wiki/Maritime_flags "Maritime flags") on the basis of durability.) In 2003 a committee of the [Scottish Parliament](/wiki/Scottish_Parliament "Scottish Parliament") recommended that the flag of Scotland use a lighter "[royal](/wiki/Royal_blue "Royal blue")" blue (Pantone 300) (the Office of the [Lord Lyon](/wiki/Lord_Lyon "Lord Lyon") does not detail specific shades of colour for use in heraldry).

A thin white stripe, or [fimbriation](/wiki/Fimbriation "Fimbriation"), separates the red cross from the blue field, in accordance with heraldry's [rule of tincture](/wiki/Rule_of_tincture "Rule of tincture") where colours (such as red and blue) must be separated from each other by metals (such as white, i.e. *[argent](/wiki/Argent "Argent")* or silver). The [blazon](/wiki/Blazon "Blazon") for the old Union Flag, to be compared with the current flag, is *azure, the cross saltire of St Andrew argent surmounted by the Cross of St George gules, fimbriated of the second.*

[![Red cross with a white border over a white saltire and dark blue background. At the centre is a gold harp in dark blue shield, which is the same size as the intersection of vertical and horizontal white bars.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/19/Flag_of_the_Commonwealth_%281658%E2%80%931660%29.svg/250px-Flag_of_the_Commonwealth_%281658%E2%80%931660%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Flag_of_the_Commonwealth_(1658%E2%80%931660).svg)

The [Protectorate Jack](/wiki/Protectorate_Jack "Protectorate Jack"), flag used by [The Protectorate](/wiki/The_Protectorate "The Protectorate") from 1658 to 1660

The [Kingdom of Ireland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Ireland "Kingdom of Ireland"), which had existed as a personal union with England since 1541, was unrepresented in the original versions of the Union Jack.[[57]](#cite_note-KnowBritain-58) However, the flag of [the Protectorate](/wiki/The_Protectorate "The Protectorate") from 1658 to 1660 was [inescutcheoned](/wiki/Escutcheon_(heraldry) "Escutcheon (heraldry)") with the [arms of Ireland](/wiki/Arms_of_Ireland "Arms of Ireland"). These were removed at [the Restoration](/wiki/English_Restoration "English Restoration"), because [Charles II](/wiki/Charles_II_of_England "Charles II of England") found them offensive.[[59]](#cite_note-60)

The original flag appears in the canton of the Commissioners' Ensign of the [Northern Lighthouse Board](/wiki/Northern_Lighthouse_Board "Northern Lighthouse Board"). This is the only contemporary official representation of the pre-1801 Union Jack in the United Kingdom[[60]](#cite_note-61) and can be seen flying from their [George Street](/wiki/George_Street,_Edinburgh "George Street, Edinburgh") headquarters in [Edinburgh](/wiki/Edinburgh "Edinburgh").

It is this version of the Union Jack that formed the [canton](/wiki/Canton_(flag) "Canton (flag)") of the [Continental Union Flag](/wiki/Continental_Union_Flag "Continental Union Flag"), the "[de facto](/wiki/De_facto "De facto")" first [national flag of the United States](/wiki/Flag_of_the_United_States "Flag of the United States") from July 1776 to June 1777.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/Command_flag%2C_Admiral_of_the_Fleet%2C_RN_%28before_1801%29_RMG_L4480.tiff/lossy-page1-250px-Command_flag%2C_Admiral_of_the_Fleet%2C_RN_%28before_1801%29_RMG_L4480.tiff.jpg)](/wiki/File:Command_flag,_Admiral_of_the_Fleet,_RN_(before_1801)_RMG_L4480.tiff)

Union Jack from [HMS *Queen Charlotte*](/wiki/HMS_Queen_Charlotte_(1790) "HMS Queen Charlotte (1790)"), the [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy") flagship during the [Glorious First of June](/wiki/Glorious_First_of_June "Glorious First of June"), 1794. As was common for the period, the flag is an approximation of the correct specification

*[Lord Howe's action, or the Glorious First of June](/wiki/Lord_Howe%27s_action,_or_the_Glorious_First_of_June "Lord Howe's action, or the Glorious First of June")*, painted in 1795, shows a Union flying from [HMS *Queen Charlotte*](/wiki/HMS_Queen_Charlotte_(1790) "HMS Queen Charlotte (1790)") on the "[Glorious First of June](/wiki/Glorious_First_of_June "Glorious First of June")" 1794. The actual flag, preserved in the [National Maritime Museum](/wiki/National_Maritime_Museum "National Maritime Museum"), is a cruder approximation of the proper specifications; this was common in 18th and early 19th century flags.[[61]](#cite_note-62)[[62]](#cite_note-63)

The flag is also flown beside [Customs House](/wiki/Customs_House,_Sydney "Customs House, Sydney") in Loftus Street, [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney"), to mark the raising of the Union Jack by [Captain Phillip](/wiki/Arthur_Phillip "Arthur Phillip") at [Sydney Cove](/wiki/Sydney_Cove "Sydney Cove"), the site of the first British settlement in [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales") and [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia") in 1788. On the plaque it is referred to as the "Jack of Queen Anne".

The flag of the [British Army](/wiki/British_Army "British Army") is the Union Jack, however in 1938 a "British Army Non-Ceremonial Flag" was devised, featuring a crowned golden lion, *statant guardant*, surmounting the royal crown, overlying two crossed swords on a red background, (the word "Army" in gold letters sometimes appears below this motif). This is not the equivalent of the ensigns of the other armed services but is used at recruiting and military or sporting events, when the army needs to be identified but the reverence and ceremony due to the [regimental](/wiki/Regiment "Regiment") flags and the Union Jack would be considered inappropriate.

#### Scottish Union Flag

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Union_Jack&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Scottish Union Flag")]

[![White saltire clearly visible over white-bordered red cross on blue background.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/BeaumontScottishUnionFlag.png/250px-BeaumontScottishUnionFlag.png)](/wiki/File:BeaumontScottishUnionFlag.png)

Scottish Union Flag depicted in the 1704 edition of *The Present State of the Universe.*

In objecting to the design of the Union Flag adopted in 1606, whereby the cross of Saint George surmounted that of Saint Andrew, a number of owners and masters of merchant vessels in the [Kingdom of Scotland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Scotland "Kingdom of Scotland") raised the issue with [John Erskine, 19th Earl of Mar](/wiki/John_Erskine,_Earl_of_Mar_(1558%E2%80%931634) "John Erskine, Earl of Mar (1558–1634)"), and were encouraged by him to send a letter of complaint to James VI, via the [Privy Council of Scotland](/wiki/Privy_Council_of_Scotland "Privy Council of Scotland"), which stated:

> Most sacred Soverayne. A greate nomber of the maisteris and awnaris of the schippis of this your Majesteis kingdome hes verie havelie compleint to your Majesteis Counsell that the form and patrone of the flaggis of schippis, send doun heir and commandit to be ressavit and used be the subjectis of boith kingdomes, is very prejudiciall to the fredome and dignitie of this Estate and will gif occasioun of reprotche to this natioun quhairevir the said flage sal happin to be worne beyond sea becaus, as your sacred majestie may persave, the Scottis Croce, callit Sanctandrois Croce is twyse divydit, and the Inglishe Croce, callit Sanct George, haldin haill and drawne through the Scottis Croce, whiche is thairby obscurit and no takin nor merk to be seen of the Scottis Armes. This will breid some heit and miscontentment betwix your Majesteis subjectis, and it is to be ferit that some inconvenientis sall fall out betwix thame, for oure seyfairing men cannot be inducit to ressave that flag as it is set doun. They haif drawne two new drauchtis and patronis as most indifferent for boith kingdomes which they present to the Counsell, and craved our approbatioun of the same; bot we haif reserved that to you Majesteis princelie determination.[[63]](#cite_note-64)

Although documents accompanying this complaint which contained drafts for alternative designs have been lost, evidence exists, at least on paper, of an unofficial Scottish variant, whereby the Scottish cross was uppermost. There is reason to think that cloth flags of this design were employed during the 17th century for unofficial use on Scottish vessels at sea.[[64]](#cite_note-65)[[65]](#cite_note-66)[[66]](#cite_note-Smith_1973-67) This flag's design is also described in the 1704 edition of *The Present State of the Universe* by [John Beaumont](/wiki/John_Beaumont_(geologist) "John Beaumont (geologist)"), which contains as an appendix *The Ensigns, Colours or Flags of the Ships at Sea: Belonging to The several Princes and States in the World*.[[67]](#cite_note-68)

[![Engraving of a castle on top of a steep hill, above the title "The North East View of Edinburgh Castle". On the castle flies a large Union Flag with Scottish saltire part of flag most visible.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/23/Edinburgh_Castle_John_Slezer_trimmed.jpg/250px-Edinburgh_Castle_John_Slezer_trimmed.jpg)](/wiki/File:Edinburgh_Castle_John_Slezer_trimmed.jpg)

[Slezer's](/wiki/John_Slezer "John Slezer") [Edinburgh Castle](/wiki/Edinburgh_Castle "Edinburgh Castle") c. 1693 depicting the Scottish Union flag[[68]](#cite_note-slezer-69)

On land, evidence confirming the use of this flag appears in the depiction of [Edinburgh Castle](/wiki/Edinburgh_Castle "Edinburgh Castle") by [John Slezer](/wiki/John_Slezer "John Slezer"), in his series of [engravings](/wiki/Engraving "Engraving") entitled *Theatrum Scotiae*, c. 1693. Appearing in later editions of *Theatrum Scotiae*, the *North East View of Edinburgh Castle* engraving depicts the *[Scotch](/wiki/Scotch_(adjective) "Scotch (adjective)")* (to use the appropriate adjective of that period) version of the Union Flag flying from the Palace block of the Castle.[[69]](#cite_note-70) On *[The North Prospect of the City of Edenburgh](/wiki/The_North_Prospect_of_the_City_of_Edenburgh "The North Prospect of the City of Edenburgh")* engraving, the flag is indistinct.[[70]](#cite_note-north_prospect-71)

On 17 April 1707, two weeks prior to the [Acts of Union](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1707 "Acts of Union 1707") coming into effect, and with Sir [Henry St George, the younger](/wiki/Henry_St_George,_the_younger "Henry St George, the younger"), the [Garter King of Arms](/wiki/Garter_King_of_Arms "Garter King of Arms"), having presented several designs of flag to [Queen Anne](/wiki/Anne,_Queen_of_Great_Britain "Anne, Queen of Great Britain") and her [Privy Council](/wiki/Privy_Council_of_England "Privy Council of England") for consideration, the flag for the soon to be unified [Kingdom of Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain") was chosen. At the suggestion of the Scots representatives, the designs for consideration included that version of Union Jack showing the Cross of Saint Andrew uppermost; identified as being the "*Scotts union flagg as said to be used by the Scotts*".[[71]](#cite_note-de_Burton-72) However, the Queen and her Council approved Sir Henry's original effort, numbered "one".[[71]](#cite_note-de_Burton-72)

A manuscript compiled in 1785 by William Fox and in possession of the [Flag Research Center](/wiki/Flag_Research_Center "Flag Research Center") includes a full plate showing "*the scoth*  [*[sic](/wiki/Sic "Sic")*] *union*" flag. This could imply that there was still some use of a Scottish variant before the addition of the cross of St Patrick to the Union Flag in 1801.[[66]](#cite_note-Smith_1973-67)

### After 1801

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Union_Jack&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: After 1801")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/British_flag_flown_from_HMS_Spartiate_during_the_Battle_of_Trafalgar.jpg/250px-British_flag_flown_from_HMS_Spartiate_during_the_Battle_of_Trafalgar.jpg)](/wiki/File:British_flag_flown_from_HMS_Spartiate_during_the_Battle_of_Trafalgar.jpg)

The Union Jack flown from [HMS *Spartiate*](/wiki/HMS_Spartiate_(1798) "HMS Spartiate (1798)") during the [Battle of Trafalgar](/wiki/Battle_of_Trafalgar "Battle of Trafalgar") (1805), showing the 1801 flag design

The current and second Union Jack dates from 1 January 1801 with the [Act of Union 1800](/wiki/Act_of_Union_1800 "Act of Union 1800"), which merged the [Kingdom of Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain") and the [Kingdom of Ireland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Ireland "Kingdom of Ireland") to form the [United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland"). The new design added a red [saltire](/wiki/Saltire "Saltire"), the [cross of Saint Patrick](/wiki/Saint_Patrick%27s_Flag "Saint Patrick's Flag") (which originates from the arms of the [Old English](/wiki/Normans_in_Ireland "Normans in Ireland") [FitzGerald family](/wiki/FitzGerald_family "FitzGerald family") rather than directly from the saint),[[72]](#cite_note-73) for Ireland. This is [counterchanged](/wiki/Counterchanging "Counterchanging") with the saltire of St Andrew, such that the red always follows the white clockwise. The arrangement has introduced a requirement to display the flag "the right way up" (see [specifications for flag use](#Specification) above). As with the red cross, so too the red saltire is separated by a white fimbriation from the blue field.[[30]](#cite_note-Flagsofworld-31) This fimbriation is repeated for symmetry on the white portion of the saltire, which thereby appears wider than the red portion. The fimbriation of the cross of St George separates its red from the red of the saltire.

#### Flag speculation after Irish Free State establishment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Union_Jack&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Flag speculation after Irish Free State establishment")]

When the [Anglo-Irish Treaty](/wiki/Anglo-Irish_Treaty "Anglo-Irish Treaty") was concluded on 6 December 1921 and the creation of the new [Irish Free State](/wiki/Irish_Free_State "Irish Free State") was an imminent prospect, the question arose as to whether the cross of Saint Patrick should remain in the Union Jack. *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* reported that on 22 January 1922:

> At the [College of Arms](/wiki/College_of_Arms "College of Arms") it was stated that certain modifications were under consideration and that if any action were taken it would be done by the King in Council. No parliamentary action would be necessary. Heraldry experts say that alterations in arms are very expensive. Some years ago there was a demand from Irish quarters that the blue ground of the golden harp on the royal standard should be changed to green. It was then estimated that the alteration would cost at least £2,000,000. To remove all reference to Ireland from the present Union Jack and Royal Arms would be vastly more expensive.[[73]](#cite_note-74)

There was some speculation on the matter in British dominions also, with one New Zealand paper reporting that:

> ...the removal of the cross of St. Patrick Cross after 120 years will transform the appearance of the flag. It will certainly become a flag under which great victories were won in the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries, but to most minds the sentimental loss will be great. Probably it will be found that the deletion is not absolutely necessary. Other possible changes include the abolition of the title of the United Kingdom, and the removal of the harp from the Royal Standard and the Coat of Arms, and the substitution of the Ulster emblem.[[74]](#cite_note-ashburton-75)

However, the fact that it was likely that [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland "Northern Ireland") would remain in the United Kingdom gave better grounds for keeping the cross of St. Patrick in the Union Jack. In this regard, [Sir James Craig](/wiki/Sir_James_Craig "Sir James Craig"), the [Prime Minister of Northern Ireland](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Northern_Ireland "Prime Minister of Northern Ireland") remarked in December 1921 that he and his government were "glad to think that our decision [to remain part of United Kingdom] will obviate the necessity of mutilating the Union Jack."[[75]](#cite_note-ulster-76) Though remaining within the United Kingdom, the new government of Northern Ireland dispensed with the St Patrick's Saltire in favour of [a new flag](/wiki/Ulster_banner "Ulster banner") derived from the [coat-of-arms of the Burkes](/wiki/House_of_Burke "House of Burke"), Earls of Ulster, and quite similar to England's St George's Cross.

Ultimately, when the British [home secretary](/wiki/Home_secretary "Home secretary") was asked on 7 December 1922 (the day after the Irish Free State was established) whether the [Garter King of Arms](/wiki/Garter_Principal_King_of_Arms "Garter Principal King of Arms") was "to issue any Regulations with reference to the national flag consequent to the passing of the Irish Free State Constitution Act", the response was no and the flag has never been changed.[[76]](#cite_note-77)

A [Dáil question](/wiki/Question_Time "Question Time") in 1961 mooted raising the removal of the cross of St Patrick with the British government; Frank Aiken, the [Irish Minister for External Affairs](/wiki/Minister_for_Foreign_Affairs_and_Trade "Minister for Foreign Affairs and Trade"), declined to "waste time on heraldic disputations".[[77]](#cite_note-78)

#### 21st century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Union_Jack&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: 21st century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/Union_Flag_%28including_Wales%29.svg/250px-Union_Flag_%28including_Wales%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Union_Flag_(including_Wales).svg)

One suggested redesign of the Union Jack with the red dragon from the [flag of Wales](/wiki/Flag_of_Wales "Flag of Wales") added in the centre

The lack of any Welsh symbol or colours in the flag is a result of Wales having been considered an integral part of the Kingdom of England at the time the [flag of Great Britain](/wiki/Flag_of_Great_Britain "Flag of Great Britain") was created in 1606. Since there is no Welsh element in the Union Jack, [Wrexham](/wiki/Wrexham "Wrexham")'s [Labour](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") [MP](/wiki/Member_of_Parliament "Member of Parliament") [Ian Lucas](/wiki/Ian_Lucas "Ian Lucas") proposed on 26 November 2007 in a [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom") debate that the Union Flag be combined with the [Welsh flag](/wiki/Flag_of_Wales "Flag of Wales") to reflect Wales's status within the UK, and that the [red dragon](/wiki/Welsh_Dragon "Welsh Dragon") be added to the Union Flag's red, white, and blue pattern.[[78]](#cite_note-welsh-79) He said the Union Jack currently only represented the other three UK nations, and [Minister for Culture, Creative Industries and Tourism](/wiki/Department_for_Culture,_Media_and_Sport "Department for Culture, Media and Sport") [Margaret Hodge](/wiki/Margaret_Hodge "Margaret Hodge") conceded that Lucas had raised a valid point for debate. She said, "the Government is keen to make the Union Flag a positive symbol of Britishness reflecting the diversity of our country today and encouraging people to take pride in our flag." This development sparked design contests with entries from all over the world.[[79]](#cite_note-face-80)

In the run-up to the [2014 Scottish independence referendum](/wiki/2014_Scottish_independence_referendum "2014 Scottish independence referendum"), various non-official suggestions were made for how the flag could be redesigned without the St Andrew's Cross if Scotland left the Union. However, as Scotland voted against independence, the issue did not arise.[[80]](#cite_note-81)[[81]](#cite_note-82)

In September 2025, the [England flag](/wiki/Flag_of_England "Flag of England") and the Union Jack became increasingly visible in the [UK](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), in what was described as a "flag phenomenon".[[82]](#cite_note-Guardian2025-09-14-83) The first major display occurred in [Weoley Castle](/wiki/Weoley_Castle "Weoley Castle"), a neighbourhood of largely postwar council stock homes, before spreading to other parts of [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham "Birmingham") in response to the 'proliferation' of [Palestine flags](/wiki/Flag_of_Palestine "Flag of Palestine").[[82]](#cite_note-Guardian2025-09-14-83)[[83]](#cite_note-Times2025-08-15-84) The phenomenon drew media attention after local authorities in [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham "Birmingham") and [York](/wiki/York "York") ordered the removal of [British flags](/wiki/Flag_of_the_United_Kingdom "Flag of the United Kingdom") while allowing [Palestine flags](/wiki/Flag_of_Palestine "Flag of Palestine") to remain, prompting public backlash and accusations of political double standards.[[82]](#cite_note-Guardian2025-09-14-83)[[83]](#cite_note-Times2025-08-15-84)

## Status in the United Kingdom

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Union_Jack&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Status in the United Kingdom")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/The_Union_Jack_Flag_MOD_45153521.jpg/250px-The_Union_Jack_Flag_MOD_45153521.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Union_Jack_Flag_MOD_45153521.jpg)

A Union Jack flying from a jackstaff onboard a Royal Navy warship, 2011

The Union Jack is used as a [jack](/wiki/Jack_(flag) "Jack (flag)") by commissioned warships and submarines of the [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy"), and by commissioned [army](/wiki/British_Army "British Army") and [Royal Air Force](/wiki/Royal_Air_Force "Royal Air Force") vessels. When at anchor or alongside, it is flown from the [jackstaff](/wiki/Jackstaff "Jackstaff") at the bow of the ship. When a ship is underway, the Union Jack is only flown from the jackstaff when the ship is dressed for a special occasion, such as the King's official birthday.

The Union Flag is worn at the masthead of a ship to indicate the presence of an [Admiral of the Fleet](/wiki/Admiral_of_the_Fleet_(Royal_Navy) "Admiral of the Fleet (Royal Navy)"), former [First Sea Lords](/wiki/First_Sea_Lord "First Sea Lord") and [Admirals](/wiki/Admiral "Admiral") who are or have been [Chief of Defence Staff](/wiki/Chief_of_the_Defence_Staff_(United_Kingdom) "Chief of the Defence Staff (United Kingdom)"). On the day on which a [court-martial](/wiki/Court-martial "Court-martial") is to sit, the Union Flag is to be hoisted at the peak or at the yardarm as appropriate and a gun is to be fired when colours are hoisted, or at the time the signal is made if the court is ordered to sit immediately.[[84]](#cite_note-FOTWsea-85)

The Royal Standard is flown when the Sovereign embarks in a warship. When other members of the Royal family embark they fly their own personal standard, which is usually a modified version of the Royal Standard.

No law has been passed making the Union Jack the national flag of the United Kingdom: it has become one through precedent. Its first recorded recognition as a national flag came in 1908, when it was stated in Parliament that "the Union Jack should be regarded as the National flag".[[85]](#cite_note-86) A more categorical statement was made by [Home Secretary](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_the_Home_Department "Secretary of State for the Home Department") [Sir John Gilmour](/wiki/Sir_John_Gilmour,_2nd_Baronet "Sir John Gilmour, 2nd Baronet"), in 1933 when he stated that "the Union Flag is the national flag and may properly be flown by any British subject on land."[[86]](#cite_note-87)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/Union_Jacks_in_Godalming_-_geograph.org.uk_-_3028373.jpg/250px-Union_Jacks_in_Godalming_-_geograph.org.uk_-_3028373.jpg)](/wiki/File:Union_Jacks_in_Godalming_-_geograph.org.uk_-_3028373.jpg)

Union Jacks on two flagpoles hanging from a building in [Surrey](/wiki/Surrey "Surrey"), 2012

Civilian use is permitted on land, but use of the unmodified flag at sea is restricted to military vessels. Unauthorised use of the flag in the 17th century to avoid paying harbour duties – a privilege restricted to naval ships – caused James's successor, [Charles I](/wiki/Charles_I_of_England "Charles I of England"), to order that use of the flag on naval vessels be restricted to His Majesty's ships "upon pain of Our high displeasure."[[23]](#cite_note-Perrin_59-24) It remains a criminal offence under the Merchant Shipping Act 1995[[87]](#cite_note-88) to display the Union Flag (other than the "pilot jack" – see below) from a British ship. Naval ships will fly the [white ensign](/wiki/White_ensign "White ensign"), merchant and private boats can fly the [red ensign](/wiki/Red_ensign "Red ensign"), others with special permission such as naval yacht clubs can fly the [blue ensign](/wiki/Blue_ensign "Blue ensign"). All of the coloured ensigns contain the union flag as part of the design.

The [Court of the Lord Lyon](/wiki/Court_of_the_Lord_Lyon "Court of the Lord Lyon"), which has legal jurisdiction in heraldic matters in Scotland, confirms that the Union Jack "is the correct flag for all citizens and corporate bodies of the United Kingdom to fly to demonstrate their loyalty and their nationality."[[88]](#cite_note-lordlyon-89)

On 5 February 2008, [Conservative](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)") [Member of Parliament](/wiki/Member_of_Parliament_(United_Kingdom) "Member of Parliament (United Kingdom)") (MP) [Andrew Rosindell](/wiki/Andrew_Rosindell "Andrew Rosindell") introduced the 'Union Flag Bill' as a [private member's bill](/wiki/Private_member%27s_bill "Private member's bill") under the [10 Minute Rule](/wiki/Ten_Minute_Rule "Ten Minute Rule") in the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom"). The Bill sought to formalise the position of the Union Flag as the national flag of the UK in law, to remove legal obstacles to its regular display. The Bill did not receive its [second reading](/wiki/Second_reading "Second reading") by the end of that [parliamentary session](/wiki/Parliamentary_session "Parliamentary session").[[89]](#cite_note-bill-90) The Bill stated "Union flag (commonly known as the Union Jack)" in subsection 1(1), but otherwise uses the term "Union Flag".[[90]](#cite_note-91)

### Flag days

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Union_Jack&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Flag days")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/The_Coldstream_Guards_Troop_Their_Colour_MOD_45165212.jpg/250px-The_Coldstream_Guards_Troop_Their_Colour_MOD_45165212.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Coldstream_Guards_Troop_Their_Colour_MOD_45165212.jpg)

Vertical displays of the Union Flag during a parade for Elizabeth II's [official birthday](/wiki/King%27s_Official_Birthday "King's Official Birthday")

In July 2007, prime minister [Gordon Brown](/wiki/Gordon_Brown "Gordon Brown") unveiled plans to have the Union Flag flown more often from government buildings.[[91]](#cite_note-92) While consultation on new guidelines was under way, the decision to fly the flag could be made by each government department.[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] In March 2021, the [UK government](/wiki/UK_government "UK government") published new guidance for the Union Flag to be flown all year round on UK government buildings, unless another flag is being flown, such as another national flag of the UK, a county flag, or other flags to mark civic pride.[[92]](#cite_note-93)

Previously, the flag was generally only flown on public buildings on days marking the birthdays of members of the [royal family](/wiki/British_royal_family "British royal family"), the [wedding anniversary](/wiki/Wedding_anniversary "Wedding anniversary") of the monarch, [Commonwealth Day](/wiki/Commonwealth_Day "Commonwealth Day"), [Accession Day](/wiki/Accession_Day "Accession Day"), [Coronation Day](/wiki/Coronation_Day "Coronation Day"), the [monarch's official birthday](/wiki/King%27s_Official_Birthday "King's Official Birthday"), [Remembrance Sunday](/wiki/Remembrance_Sunday "Remembrance Sunday"), and on the days of the State Opening and [prorogation](/wiki/Legislative_session#Common_procedure "Legislative session") of Parliament. Non-government organisations were (and are) permitted to fly the Union Flag whenever they choose.

Current guidance from the UK Government, first published in February 2013 and last updated in January 2026, states that governmental buildings in the UK are encouraged to fly the Union Flag all year round. It also specifies specific days when the flag must be flown from these buildings.[[93]](#cite_note-FlagFlyingUKGov-94)

As of March 2026[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Union_Jack&action=edit), the specified set of days when the Union Flag must be flown from government buildings throughout the UK are:[[93]](#cite_note-FlagFlyingUKGov-94)

* Second Monday in March ([Commonwealth Day](/wiki/Commonwealth_Day "Commonwealth Day"))
* 9 April (anniversary of the [wedding of The King and The Queen](/wiki/Wedding_of_Prince_Charles_and_Camilla_Parker_Bowles "Wedding of Prince Charles and Camilla Parker Bowles"))
* 6 May (anniversary of the [coronation of The King and The Queen](/wiki/Coronation_of_Charles_III_and_Camilla "Coronation of Charles III and Camilla"))
* Second Saturday in June ([official birthday of The King](/wiki/King%27s_Official_Birthday "King's Official Birthday"))
* 21 June (birthday of [The Prince of Wales](/wiki/William,_Prince_of_Wales "William, Prince of Wales"))
* 17 July (birthday of [The Queen](/wiki/Queen_Camilla "Queen Camilla"))
* 8 September (anniversary of the accession of [The King](/wiki/Charles_III "Charles III"))
* Second Sunday in November ([Remembrance Sunday](/wiki/Remembrance_Sunday "Remembrance Sunday"))
* 14 November (actual birthday of The King)

In addition, the flag must be flown on government buildings in the following areas on these days:[[93]](#cite_note-FlagFlyingUKGov-94)

* [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales"), 1 March: [Saint David's Day](/wiki/Saint_David%27s_Day "Saint David's Day")
* [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland "Northern Ireland"), 17 March: [Saint Patrick's Day](/wiki/Saint_Patrick%27s_Day "Saint Patrick's Day")
* [England](/wiki/England "England"), 23 April: [Saint George's Day](/wiki/Saint_George%27s_Day "Saint George's Day")
* [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland"), 30 November: [Saint Andrew's Day](/wiki/Saint_Andrew%27s_Day "Saint Andrew's Day")
* [Greater London](/wiki/Greater_London "Greater London"): the opening and proroguing of Parliament

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2b/Union_Jack_at_half_mast%2C_Island_Harbour_marina%2C_Isle_of_Wight%2C_England_%282%29.jpg/250px-Union_Jack_at_half_mast%2C_Island_Harbour_marina%2C_Isle_of_Wight%2C_England_%282%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Union_Jack_at_half_mast,_Island_Harbour_marina,_Isle_of_Wight,_England_(2).jpg)

A Union Jack at half-mast after the [death of Elizabeth II](/wiki/Death_of_Elizabeth_II "Death of Elizabeth II") in September 2022

The Union Flag is flown at [half-mast](/wiki/Half-mast "Half-mast") from the announcement of the death of the sovereign (save for Proclamation Day), or upon command of the sovereign.[[94]](#cite_note-dcms-95)

On 30 November, [St Andrew's Day](/wiki/Saint_Andrew%27s_Day "Saint Andrew's Day"), the Union Flag can be flown in Scotland only where a building has more than one flagpole—on this day the Saltire will not be lowered to make way for the Union Flag if there is only one flagpole.[[95]](#cite_note-scotland-96) This difference arose after [members of the Scottish Parliament](/wiki/Member_of_the_Scottish_Parliament "Member of the Scottish Parliament") complained that Scotland was the only country in the world that could not fly its national flag on its national day. However, on 23 April, St George's Day, it is the Union Flag of the United Kingdom that is flown over UK government offices in England.[[96]](#cite_note-agree-97)

### Usage and disposal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Union_Jack&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Usage and disposal")]

The Union Flag has no official status in the United Kingdom, and there are no national regulations concerning its use or prohibitions against [flag desecration](/wiki/Flag_desecration "Flag desecration"). In Northern Ireland, the [Flags Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2000](/w/index.php?title=Flags_Regulations_(Northern_Ireland)_2000&action=edit&redlink=1 "Flags Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2000 (page does not exist)") provide for the flying of the flag on government buildings on certain occasions, when it is flown half-mast, and how it is displayed with other flags.[[97]](#cite_note-98)

The Flags and Heraldry Committee, an [all-party parliamentary group](/wiki/All-party_parliamentary_group "All-party parliamentary group") lobbying for official standards, cooperated with the [Flag Institute](/wiki/Flag_Institute "Flag Institute") in 2010 to publish a set of recommended guidelines for the flag's display and use as a symbol.[[98]](#cite_note-FFitUK-99)

There is no specific way in which the Union Flag should be folded. It is usually folded rectilinearly, with the hoist on the outside, to be easily reattached to the pole.[[98]](#cite_note-FFitUK-99)[[99]](#cite_note-fotw-canada-100)

Royal Navy Stores Duties Instructions, article 447, dated 26 February 1914, specified that flags condemned from further service use were to be torn up into small pieces and disposed of as rags, not to be used for decoration or sold. The exception was flags that had flown in action: these could be framed and kept on board, or transferred to a "suitable place", such as a museum.[[30]](#cite_note-Flagsofworld-31)

#### Position of Honour

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Union_Jack&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Position of Honour")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d6/Gibraltar_GM_2008_%2861%29.JPG/250px-Gibraltar_GM_2008_%2861%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:Gibraltar_GM_2008_(61).JPG)

The Union Jack in a raised, central position, flanked by the [Flag of Europe](/wiki/Flag_of_Europe "Flag of Europe") (left) and [Flag of Gibraltar](/wiki/Flag_of_Gibraltar "Flag of Gibraltar")

According to the UK Flag Protocol, the order of precedence of flags in the United Kingdom is: the [Royal Standards](/wiki/Royal_Standard_of_the_United_Kingdom "Royal Standard of the United Kingdom"), the Union Flag, the flag of the host country (England, Scotland and Wales etc.), the flags of other nations (in [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") [alphabetical order](/wiki/Alphabetical_order "Alphabetical order")), the [Commonwealth Flag](/wiki/Flag_of_the_Commonwealth_of_Nations "Flag of the Commonwealth of Nations"), the county flags, the flags of cities or towns, the banners of arms, and the house flags.[[100]](#cite_note-101)

#### British Overseas Territories and Crown Dependencies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Union_Jack&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: British Overseas Territories and Crown Dependencies")]

The Union Jack is the national flag of the [British Overseas Territories](/wiki/British_Overseas_Territories "British Overseas Territories"), which are parts of the British realm with varying degrees of local autonomy. Most populated administrative regions and territories of the United Kingdom have been granted a unique flag for the locality, usually the [Blue ensign](/wiki/Blue_ensign "Blue ensign") or [Red ensign](/wiki/Red_ensign "Red ensign") defaced with the distinguishing arms of the territory. All fly the Union Jack in some form, with the exception of [Gibraltar](/wiki/Flag_of_Gibraltar "Flag of Gibraltar") (other than the government ensign).

The [Crown Dependencies](/wiki/Crown_Dependencies "Crown Dependencies"), unlike the British Overseas Territories, are legally not part of the United Kingdom, and the Union Jack is not an official flag there. Outside the UK, the Union Jack is usually part of a special [ensign](/wiki/Ensign_(flag) "Ensign (flag)") in which it is placed in the upper left hand corner of a blue field, with a signifying crest in the bottom right. When the Union Jack and the territorial flag are flown together, the national flag is always arranged to take precedence over the territorial flag.[[101]](#cite_note-102)

## Status outside the United Kingdom

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Union_Jack&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Status outside the United Kingdom")]

### Australia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Union_Jack&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Australia")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c8/SLNSW_6429_First_Australianborn_Governor_General_Sir_Isaac_Isaccs_takes_the_salute_from_the_steps_of_the_then_new_Parliament_House_his_AidedeCamp_Captain_Bracegirdle_on_left_the_press_photographer_is_Alec_Bell.jpg/250px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:SLNSW_6429_First_Australianborn_Governor_General_Sir_Isaac_Isaccs_takes_the_salute_from_the_steps_of_the_then_new_Parliament_House_his_AidedeCamp_Captain_Bracegirdle_on_left_the_press_photographer_is_Alec_Bell.jpg)

The [Australian ensign](/wiki/Flag_of_Australia "Flag of Australia") and Union Jack hung vertically from Australia's [Parliament House](/wiki/Old_Parliament_House,_Canberra "Old Parliament House, Canberra"), 1931

The Union Flag was used formally as the Australian national flag until 1953, having official precedence over the [Australian Blue Ensign](/wiki/Flag_of_Australia "Flag of Australia"), though the latter was used by government and informally.[[102]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKwan200610-103) Schools were encouraged to fly the Union Jack to encourage patriotism for the British Empire, with South Australia requiring students from 1911 to 1956 to fly the Union Jack for the "national salute".[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKwan20068-104)

Over time, the blue ensign came to be used and embraced as the national flag, officially being designated as the *Australian National Flag* in 1953 with the passage of the *[Flags Act](/wiki/Flags_Act_1953 "Flags Act 1953").[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKwan20068-104)* Due to the sensitivity of those who still considered the Union Jack the national flag, the act specified that it did "not affect the right or privilege of a person to fly the Union Jack".[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKwan2006106-105) Additionally, Australian prime minister [Robert Menzies](/wiki/Robert_Menzies "Robert Menzies") told Australians that the Union Flag would be flown together with the Australian national flag "on notable occasions".[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKwan20068-104) The Union Jack continued to see informal use as a flag of Australia for a period thereafter, although by the 1980s, the majority of Australians viewed the Australian blue ensign as the national flag as opposed to the Union Jack.[[105]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKwan20066-106)

### Canada

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Union_Jack&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Canada")]

Further information: [List of Canadian flags](/wiki/List_of_Canadian_flags "List of Canadian flags")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/North-West_Mounted_Police_standing_next_to_American_and_British_flags_marking_the_boundary_between_Alaska_and_British_Columbia_%28HEGG_446%29.jpeg/250px-North-West_Mounted_Police_standing_next_to_American_and_British_flags_marking_the_boundary_between_Alaska_and_British_Columbia_%28HEGG_446%29.jpeg)](/wiki/File:North-West_Mounted_Police_standing_next_to_American_and_British_flags_marking_the_boundary_between_Alaska_and_British_Columbia_(HEGG_446).jpeg)

The Union Flag and the [U.S. flag](/wiki/Flag_of_the_United_States "Flag of the United States") at the [Canada-U.S. border](/wiki/Canada%E2%80%93United_States_border "Canada–United States border"), 1899. The Union Flag was the formal flag of Canada until 1965.

The Union Jack was the official national [flag of Canada](/wiki/Flag_of_Canada "Flag of Canada") until 1965 when it was replaced in that role by the Maple Leaf flag.[[106]](#cite_note-pch-107)[[107]](#cite_note-can3-108) Since 1965, the Union Jack in Canada is used as an authorised symbol to represent Canada's "membership in the [Commonwealth of Nations](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations") and its allegiance to [the Crown](/wiki/The_Crown "The Crown")".[[108]](#cite_note-ruf2-109)

#### History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Union_Jack&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: History")]

The Union Jack has been used in a variety of colonies in [British North America](/wiki/British_North_America "British North America") since its official adoption in 1707, In 1867, the British North American colonies of [New Brunswick](/wiki/New_Brunswick "New Brunswick"), [Nova Scotia](/wiki/Nova_Scotia "Nova Scotia"), and the [Province of Canada](/wiki/Province_of_Canada "Province of Canada") were united to form the [Canadian Confederation](/wiki/Canadian_Confederation "Canadian Confederation"); with the Union Jack being retained as the official flag of the new "confederation".[[106]](#cite_note-pch-107)

In addition to the Union Jack, during the late 19th century, a red ensign defaced with the [arms of Canada](/wiki/Arms_of_Canada "Arms of Canada") was also used as an informal flag of Canada. The defaced red ensign, later known as the [Canadian Red Ensign](/wiki/Canadian_Red_Ensign "Canadian Red Ensign"), was eventually authorised for official use as the country's civil ensign in 1892. Although the Canadian Red Ensign was only formally authorised as a civil ensign, it was also viewed as a *de facto* national flag, as it was a uniquely Canadian symbol.[[106]](#cite_note-pch-107)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/VE_Day_celebrations_on_Bay_Street_1945.jpg/250px-VE_Day_celebrations_on_Bay_Street_1945.jpg)](/wiki/File:VE_Day_celebrations_on_Bay_Street_1945.jpg)

[VE Day](/wiki/VE_Day "VE Day") celebrations in [Toronto](/wiki/Toronto "Toronto") in 1945, with several buildings flying the Union Jack and several people waving the flag.

In 1964, Canadian Prime Minister [Lester B. Pearson](/wiki/Lester_B._Pearson "Lester B. Pearson") introduced plans to replace the Union Flag with a new national flag, spurring the [Great Canadian flag debate](/wiki/Great_Canadian_flag_debate "Great Canadian flag debate").[[106]](#cite_note-pch-107) A new national flag, the *Maple Leaf*, was approved by the [parliament of Canada](/wiki/Parliament_of_Canada "Parliament of Canada") on 18 December 1964.[[106]](#cite_note-pch-107)[[109]](#cite_note-ggcanada-110) However, on the following day, the Canadian parliament passed another resolution that designated the Union Flag as the *Royal Union Flag* and authorised its official use as the symbol of the country's membership in the Commonwealth of Nations and its allegiance to the Crown.[[108]](#cite_note-ruf2-109) On 15 February 1965, the maple leaf flag formally replaced the Union Flag as the flag of Canada following an official proclamation by [Elizabeth II](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II"),[[106]](#cite_note-pch-107) with the Royal Union Flag becoming an official ceremonial flag.[[108]](#cite_note-ruf2-109) The Royal Union Flag's specific use as approved by parliament was registered with the [Canadian Heraldic Authority](/wiki/Canadian_Heraldic_Authority "Canadian Heraldic Authority") in May 2005.[[109]](#cite_note-ggcanada-110)

The Union Flag was also legislated as the national flag for the [Dominion of Newfoundland](/wiki/Dominion_of_Newfoundland "Dominion of Newfoundland") in 1931, a separate [dominion](/wiki/Dominion "Dominion") of the British Empire. [Newfoundland](/wiki/Newfoundland_and_Labrador "Newfoundland and Labrador") retained the Union Flag as a provincial flag after it joined the Canadian confederation in 1949, reaffirmed through the 1952 Revised Statutes of Newfoundland. In 1980, the [flag of Newfoundland](/wiki/Flag_of_Newfoundland_and_Labrador "Flag of Newfoundland and Labrador") was adopted as the new provincial flag, with the design for the new flag of Newfoundland being derived from the Union Flag.[[110]](#cite_note-111)

#### Protocol

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Union_Jack&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Protocol")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/Ottawa_on_Commonwealth_Day_%28March_14%29_2022.jpg/250px-Ottawa_on_Commonwealth_Day_%28March_14%29_2022.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ottawa_on_Commonwealth_Day_(March_14)_2022.jpg)

The Royal Union Flag in [Ottawa](/wiki/Ottawa "Ottawa") on [Commonwealth Day](/wiki/Commonwealth_Day "Commonwealth Day") in 2022

The parliamentary resolution passed on 18 December 1964 assigned two purposes for the Royal Union Flag: a flag representing the United Kingdom and an official ceremonial flag of Canada.[[107]](#cite_note-can3-108) When used to represent the United Kingdom, the flag takes precedence before the flag of a Canadian province or territory. However, when the flag is used as a ceremonial flag of Canada, the flag of a Canadian province or territory takes precedence before the Royal Union Flag.[[107]](#cite_note-can3-108)

The parliamentary resolution requires the Royal Union Flag to be flown alongside the national flag of Canada (if there are at least two flag poles available) on federal properties on [Commonwealth Day](/wiki/Commonwealth_Day "Commonwealth Day"), [Victoria Day](/wiki/Victoria_Day "Victoria Day") (the [monarch's official birthday](/wiki/King%27s_Official_Birthday#Canada "King's Official Birthday") in Canada), 11 December (the anniversary of the enactment of the [Statute of Westminster, 1931](/wiki/Statute_of_Westminster_1931 "Statute of Westminster 1931")), and when otherwise instructed to do so by the [National Defence Headquarters](/wiki/National_Defence_Headquarters "National Defence Headquarters").[[108]](#cite_note-ruf2-109)[[107]](#cite_note-can3-108) The Royal Union Flag may also be formally flown alongside the flag of Canada at federal locations in Canada for ceremonies, anniversaries, and other events relating to the [Canadian Armed Forces](/wiki/Canadian_Armed_Forces "Canadian Armed Forces") or other forces in the Commonwealth.[[108]](#cite_note-ruf2-109)[[107]](#cite_note-can3-108)

Items and properties that the parliamentary resolution applies to includes buildings operated by the federal government, military installations, federally-operated airports, at the masthead of [Royal Canadian Navy](/wiki/Royal_Canadian_Navy "Royal Canadian Navy") ships within Canadian waters, and other appropriate establishments. The requirement for federal properties to fly the Royal Union Flag applies only when there are two or more flagpoles on the property, to ensure that the national flag of Canada is not taken down in place of the Royal Union Flag.[[108]](#cite_note-ruf2-109)[[107]](#cite_note-can3-108)[[111]](#cite_note-112)

### New Zealand

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Union_Jack&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: New Zealand")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/1888-1889_Natives_Tour.jpg/250px-1888-1889_Natives_Tour.jpg)](/wiki/File:1888-1889_Natives_Tour.jpg)

The [1888–89 New Zealand Native football team](/wiki/1888%E2%80%9389_New_Zealand_Native_football_team "1888–89 New Zealand Native football team") pose in front of the [flag of the United Tribes of New Zealand](/wiki/Flag_of_the_United_Tribes_of_New_Zealand "Flag of the United Tribes of New Zealand") and the Union Jack, 1889

The Union Flag became the flag of New Zealand after the [Treaty of Waitangi](/wiki/Treaty_of_Waitangi "Treaty of Waitangi") was signed in February 1840, replacing the flag used by the [United Tribes of New Zealand](/wiki/United_Tribes_of_New_Zealand "United Tribes of New Zealand").[[112]](#cite_note-nzflag-113) The issue of flying the flag of the United Tribes alongside the Union Jack, as a symbol of their equal standing with the colonial government, was a factor that led to the [Flagstaff War](/wiki/Flagstaff_War "Flagstaff War"), led by [Ngāpuhi](/wiki/Ng%C4%81puhi "Ngāpuhi") chief [Hōne Heke](/wiki/H%C5%8Dne_Heke "Hōne Heke").[[112]](#cite_note-nzflag-113) British maritime flags were used by New Zealand vessels until 1865.[[112]](#cite_note-nzflag-113) After the passage of the [Colonial Naval Defence Act 1865](/wiki/Colonial_Naval_Defence_Act_1865 "Colonial Naval Defence Act 1865"), vessels of the New Zealand government used a defaced [blue ensign](/wiki/Blue_ensign "Blue ensign") issued by the colonial government.

The [current national flag of New Zealand](/wiki/Flag_of_New_Zealand "Flag of New Zealand") was given official standing under the New Zealand Ensign Act in 1902, replacing the Union Flag.[[112]](#cite_note-nzflag-113)[[113]](#cite_note-114) However, the Union Flag continued to see tandem use with the national flag of New Zealand into the 1950s.[[112]](#cite_note-nzflag-113)

### South Africa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Union_Jack&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: South Africa")]

The Union Jack was used as the flag of a variety of colonies in South Africa since 1795. The Union Jack was retained as the official flag of the [Union of South Africa](/wiki/Union_of_South_Africa "Union of South Africa") after its formation in 1910. In addition to the Union Jack, from 1910 to 1928, the [South African Red Ensign](/wiki/South_African_Red_Ensign "South African Red Ensign") was also treated as an unofficial flag of the union.

Proposals to adopt a national flag were made during the 1920s. In 1927, the Union Nationality and Flag Act was passed by the [Parliament of South Africa](/wiki/Parliament_of_South_Africa "Parliament of South Africa"), which named both the Union Jack and the *[Oranje, Blanje, Blou](/wiki/Flag_of_South_Africa_(1928%E2%80%931994) "Flag of South Africa (1928–1994)")* flag of South Africa as the flags of the union, both co-equal in status. The *Oranje, Blanje, Blou* flag also incorporated the Union Jack in its design, alongside the [flag of the Orange Free State](/wiki/Flag_of_the_Orange_Free_State "Flag of the Orange Free State") and the [flag of the South African Republic](/wiki/Flag_of_the_South_African_Republic "Flag of the South African Republic"). The Union Jack was to be flown alongside the *Oranje, Blanje, Blou* at principal government buildings in the capitals, at Union ports, on government offices abroad, and at such other places as the government might determine.[[114]](#cite_note-unfa-115)[[115]](#cite_note-sava-116) The act went into effect on 31 May 1928.

Instructions issued in 1931 confirmed the places where both flags were to be flown. In addition to those already mentioned, they were the [Union Buildings](/wiki/Union_Buildings "Union Buildings") in Pretoria, the head offices of the four provincial administrations, the [supreme courts](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_South_Africa "Supreme Court of South Africa"), certain [magistrates' courts](/wiki/Magistrate%27s_court_(South_Africa) "Magistrate's court (South Africa)"), customs houses, and three buildings in [Durban](/wiki/Durban "Durban") (the general post office, the [railway station](/wiki/Durban_railway_station "Durban railway station"), and the local [military district headquarters](/wiki/Natal_Command "Natal Command")).[[116]](#cite_note-iff31-117)[[115]](#cite_note-sava-116) Under these arrangements, the Union Jack was subordinate to the *Oranje, Blanje, Blou*. As the two flags had to be the same size, it meant that the Union Jack was made in the ratio 2:3 rather than the usual 1:2.[[116]](#cite_note-iff31-117)[[115]](#cite_note-sava-116)

This dual arrangement continued until 1957, when the Flags Amendment Act was passed naming the *Oranje, Blanje, Blou* as the sole flag of South Africa.[[117]](#cite_note-faa-118) The *Oranje, Blanje, Blou* was replaced by the [flag of South Africa](/wiki/Flag_of_South_Africa "Flag of South Africa") in 1994 as the country's national flag.

### Use outside the Commonwealth

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Union_Jack&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Use outside the Commonwealth")]

Movements in countries not a part of the [Commonwealth of Nations](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations") have adopted the Union Flag as a flag of protest.

#### Hong Kong

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Union_Jack&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Hong Kong")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1c/2014_Hong_Kong_new_year_march_11.jpg/250px-2014_Hong_Kong_new_year_march_11.jpg)](/wiki/File:2014_Hong_Kong_new_year_march_11.jpg)

The Union Jack alongside the [colonial Hong Kong flag](/wiki/Flag_of_Hong_Kong_(1959%E2%80%931997) "Flag of Hong Kong (1959–1997)"), during the 2014 [new year march](/wiki/Hong_Kong_new_year_marches "Hong Kong new year marches") in Hong Kong

The Union Flag was formerly used in Hong Kong when it was a [British Dependent Territory](/wiki/British_Overseas_Territory "British Overseas Territory"). Official use of the Union Flag and the [British colonial Hong Kong flag](/wiki/Flag_of_Hong_Kong_(1959%E2%80%931997) "Flag of Hong Kong (1959–1997)") ceased following the [handover of Hong Kong](/wiki/Handover_of_Hong_Kong "Handover of Hong Kong") to China in July 1997. In the 2010s, the Union Flag, along with the colonial flag of Hong Kong began to see use by supporters of the [pro-democracy camp](/wiki/Pro-democracy_camp_(Hong_Kong) "Pro-democracy camp (Hong Kong)") during the [2014 Hong Kong protests](/wiki/2014_Hong_Kong_protests "2014 Hong Kong protests"), and the [2019–20 Hong Kong protests](/wiki/2019%E2%80%9320_Hong_Kong_protests "2019–20 Hong Kong protests").[[118]](#cite_note-reuters-119)[[119]](#cite_note-120) The flag has been displayed at other pro-democracy events in Hong Kong, including the [new year marches](/wiki/Hong_Kong_new_year_marches "Hong Kong new year marches") and the [1 July marches](/wiki/Hong_Kong_1_July_marches "Hong Kong 1 July marches"). Members of the [Hong Kong Autonomy Movement](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Autonomy_Movement "Hong Kong Autonomy Movement"), the [Hong Kong independence](/wiki/Hong_Kong_independence "Hong Kong independence") movement and [Hong Kong localists](/wiki/Localism_in_Hong_Kong "Localism in Hong Kong") have been seen wielding the Union Flag or the colonial flag of Hong Kong.[[120]](#cite_note-hkfp-121)

However, the meaning behind the use of the flags by pro-democracy protestors, including the Union Flag, remains disputed with protestors citing a variety of reasons for flying it.[[121]](#cite_note-scmp-122) Some pro-democracy protestors that flew foreign flags, including the Union Flag, did so in an effort to attract international media attention to the protests, while others did so in an effort to irritate the central government of China.[[121]](#cite_note-scmp-122)[[122]](#cite_note-123) The Union Flag, in addition to other foreign flags, were also used by some protestors to illustrate their desire for Hong Kong to be an "international city"; whereas others used the flag simply as a generic symbol of freedom.[[121]](#cite_note-scmp-122)[[123]](#cite_note-124)[[124]](#cite_note-125)

Some specifically flew the Union Flag and the colonial flag of Hong Kong, nostalgic of the "values" of the previous colonial government, namely "personal freedoms, rule of law, [and] clean governance".[[118]](#cite_note-reuters-119)[[120]](#cite_note-hkfp-121) Other pro-democracy protestors choose to use the Union Flag and the colonial flag of Hong Kong in an effort to call upon the British government to declare that China had failed to uphold the [Sino-British Joint Declaration](/wiki/Sino-British_Joint_Declaration "Sino-British Joint Declaration").[[121]](#cite_note-scmp-122) Several Hongkongers that hold [British National (Overseas)](/wiki/British_National_(Overseas) "British National (Overseas)") passports who used the flag during the protests were doing so as a call to the British government to grant British National (Overseas) the right to abode in the United Kingdom.[[121]](#cite_note-scmp-122) Although a small number of Hongkongers seek direct British intervention into the matter, the majority of those that used the Union Flag or the colonial flag of Hong Kong during the protests do not hold such beliefs.[[120]](#cite_note-hkfp-121)

The use of foreign flags at the protests, including the Union Flag, has been cited multiple times by the central government of China as evidence for their claim that foreign interference is steering the protests in Hong Kong against the central government.[[121]](#cite_note-scmp-122) Conversely, several protestors in the pro-democracy camp have also criticised the use of foreign flags, who view their use as reinforcing the claims made by the central government of China.[[121]](#cite_note-scmp-122)

#### Italy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Union_Jack&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Italy")]

After the [British referendum](/wiki/2016_United_Kingdom_European_Union_membership_referendum "2016 United Kingdom European Union membership referendum") on membership of the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") resulted in a vote to [leave](/wiki/Brexit "Brexit"), the Union Flag became a symbol of [euroscepticism](/wiki/Euroscepticism "Euroscepticism") in Italy. In August 2016, many local businesses along the [Italian riviera](/wiki/Italian_Riviera "Italian Riviera") hoisted the flags as a protest against the implementation of the [Services in the Internal Market Directive 2006](/wiki/Services_in_the_Internal_Market_Directive_2006 "Services in the Internal Market Directive 2006").[[125]](#cite_note-Ghiglione2-126)

As the national flag of the entire British Realm, the Union Flag was found in the *[canton](/wiki/Canton_(flag) "Canton (flag)")* (upper flagpole-side quarter) of the [flags](/wiki/Historical_flags_of_the_British_Empire_and_the_overseas_territories "Historical flags of the British Empire and the overseas territories") of many colonies of Britain, while the *[field](/wiki/Field_(heraldry) "Field (heraldry)")* (background) of their flags was the colour of the [naval ensign](/wiki/British_ensigns#History "British ensigns") flown by the particular [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy") squadron that patrolled that region of the world. Nations and colonies that have used the Union Flag at some stage have included [Aden](/wiki/Aden "Aden"), [Basutoland](/wiki/Basutoland "Basutoland") (now Lesotho), [Barbados](/wiki/Barbados "Barbados"), [Bechuanaland](/wiki/Bechuanaland_Protectorate "Bechuanaland Protectorate") (now Botswana), [Borneo](/wiki/Borneo "Borneo"), [Burma](/wiki/British_rule_in_Burma "British rule in Burma"), [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), [Ceylon](/wiki/British_Ceylon "British Ceylon") (now Sri Lanka), [Cyprus](/wiki/British_Cyprus "British Cyprus"), [Dominica](/wiki/Dominica "Dominica"), [British East Africa (Kenya Colony)](/wiki/British_East_Africa "British East Africa"), [Gambia](/wiki/Gambia "Gambia"), [Gold Coast (Ghana)](/wiki/Gold_Coast_(British_colony) "Gold Coast (British colony)"), [Grenada](/wiki/Grenada "Grenada"), [Guiana](/wiki/British_Guiana "British Guiana"), [Hong Kong](/wiki/British_Hong_Kong "British Hong Kong"), [Jamaica](/wiki/Colony_of_Jamaica "Colony of Jamaica"), [Labuan (Malaysia)](/wiki/Labuan "Labuan"), [Lagos](/wiki/Lagos "Lagos"), [Malta](/wiki/Crown_Colony_of_Malta "Crown Colony of Malta"), [Mauritius](/wiki/British_Mauritius "British Mauritius"), [Nigeria](/wiki/Colonial_Nigeria "Colonial Nigeria"), [Palestine](/wiki/Mandatory_Palestine "Mandatory Palestine"), [Penang (Malaysia)](/wiki/Penang "Penang"), [Rhodesia](/wiki/Southern_Rhodesia "Southern Rhodesia") (now Zimbabwe), [Sierra Leone](/wiki/Sierra_Leone_Colony_and_Protectorate "Sierra Leone Colony and Protectorate"), [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore"), [Somaliland](/wiki/British_Somaliland "British Somaliland"), [South Africa](/wiki/Union_of_South_Africa "Union of South Africa"), [Anglo-Egyptian Sudan](/wiki/Anglo-Egyptian_Sudan "Anglo-Egyptian Sudan"), [Pre-partitioned India](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj") (present-day [India](/wiki/India "India"), [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan"), [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh"), and [Myanmar](/wiki/Myanmar "Myanmar")), [Tanganyika](/wiki/Tanganyika_(territory) "Tanganyika (territory)"), [Trinidad and Tobago](/wiki/Trinidad_and_Tobago "Trinidad and Tobago"), [Uganda](/wiki/Uganda_Protectorate "Uganda Protectorate"), the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), and [Weihaiwei](/wiki/Weihaiwei_under_British_rule "Weihaiwei under British rule"). As former British Empire nations were granted independence, these and other versions of the Union Flag were decommissioned. The most recent decommissioning of the Union Flag came on 1 July 1997, when the former Dependent Territory of Hong Kong was handed over to the People's Republic of China.

Four former British colonies in Oceania which are now independent countries incorporate the Union Jack as part of their national flags: Australia, New Zealand and [Tuvalu](/wiki/Tuvalu "Tuvalu"), which have retained the monarchy; and [Fiji](/wiki/Fiji "Fiji"), which [abolished the monarchy in 1987](/wiki/1987_Fijian_coups_d%27%C3%A9tat "1987 Fijian coups d'état").

National flags of sovereign states whose design incorporates a Union Jack in their canton

In former British colonies, the Union Jack was used interchangeably with informal flags of the territory for significant parts of their colonial early history. The Union Flag was used as the flag of Canada until it was re-adopted as a ceremonial flag, and the Maple Leaf flag made the official national flag in 1965. In addition to being an official ceremonial flag, the Union Flag also defaces the flags of a number of Canadian provinces, including [British Columbia](/wiki/British_Columbia "British Columbia"), [Manitoba](/wiki/Manitoba "Manitoba"), and [Ontario](/wiki/Ontario "Ontario"). [Newfoundland and Labrador](/wiki/Newfoundland_and_Labrador "Newfoundland and Labrador") uses a flag that was derived from the Union Flag, with the Union Jack serving as the flag of Newfoundland until 1980. The Union Flag, and flags defaced with the Union Flag in its canton, similar to the Canadian Red Ensign, continue to see use in Canada in a private capacity. The pre-1801 Union Flag also sees limited use by private organisations, most notably the [United Empire Loyalists](/wiki/United_Empire_Loyalists "United Empire Loyalists") Association of Canada.[[126]](#cite_note-127)

Along with the national flag, many other [Australian](/wiki/List_of_Australian_flags "List of Australian flags") flags retain the use of the Union Jack, including the [Royal Australian Navy Ensign](/wiki/Royal_Australian_Navy_Ensign "Royal Australian Navy Ensign") (also known as the Australian White Ensign), the [Royal Australian Air Force Ensign](/wiki/Royal_Australian_Air_Force_Ensign "Royal Australian Air Force Ensign"), the [Australian Red Ensign](/wiki/Australian_Red_Ensign "Australian Red Ensign") (for use by merchant and private vessels), and the [Australian Civil Aviation Ensign](/wiki/Australian_Civil_Aviation_Ensign "Australian Civil Aviation Ensign"). The flags of all six Australian States retain the Union Jack in the canton, as do some regional flags such as the Upper and Lower [Murray River Flags](/wiki/Murray_River_Flag "Murray River Flag"). The Vice-Regal flags of the State Governors also use the Union Jack. While the [Flags Act 1953](/wiki/Flags_Act_1953 "Flags Act 1953") states that Australians still have the "right or privilege" to fly the Union Jack after the introduction of the Australian National Flag, usage of the Union Jack by itself is unusual. The unofficial flag of [Lord Howe Island](/wiki/Lord_Howe_Island "Lord Howe Island") harks to the pre-1801 Union Jack.[[127]](#cite_note-128)

The [Basque Country's](/wiki/Basque_Country_(historical_territory) "Basque Country (historical territory)") flag, the [Ikurriña](/wiki/Ikurri%C3%B1a "Ikurriña"), is also loosely based on the Union Jack, reflecting the significant commercial ties between [Bilbao](/wiki/Bilbao "Bilbao") and Britain at the time the [Ikurriña](/wiki/Ikurri%C3%B1a "Ikurriña") was designed in 1894. The [Miskito people](/wiki/Miskito_people "Miskito people") sometimes use a similar flag that also incorporates the Union Jack in its canton, due to long periods of contact in the [Mosquito Coast](/wiki/Mosquito_Coast "Mosquito Coast").

Former national flags that incorporated the Union Jack in their design

The Union Jack was used by the United States in its first flag, the Continental Union Flag. This flag was of a similar design to the one used by the [British East India Company](/wiki/British_East_India_Company "British East India Company"). The historical [Kingdom of Hawaii](/wiki/Hawaiian_Kingdom "Hawaiian Kingdom") adopted a flag featuring a Union Jack which was retained when [Hawaii](/wiki/Hawaii "Hawaii") became a US state in 1959. Hawaii's flag represents the only current use of the Union Jack in any American state flag.

Also in the United States, the Union Flag of 1606 is incorporated into the [flag of Baton Rouge](/wiki/Flag_of_Baton_Rouge "Flag of Baton Rouge"), the capital city of Louisiana. Baton Rouge was a British colony from the time of the [Seven Years' War](/wiki/Seven_Years%27_War "Seven Years' War") until the end of the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War"), when it was captured by Spanish forces. Symbols from the colonial powers France and Spain are also incorporated into the Baton Rouge flag. [Taunton, Massachusetts](/wiki/Taunton,_Massachusetts "Taunton, Massachusetts"), uses [a defaced Red Ensign variant](/wiki/Flag_of_Taunton,_Massachusetts "Flag of Taunton, Massachusetts") featuring the old-style Union Jack and has since 1974, as does [Weymouth Township, New Jersey](/wiki/Weymouth_Township,_New_Jersey "Weymouth Township, New Jersey").[[128]](#cite_note-CRW-129)[[129]](#cite_note-130) [Westmoreland County, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Westmoreland_County,_Pennsylvania "Westmoreland County, Pennsylvania"), has been known to fly a flag containing the King's Colours since 1973.[[130]](#cite_note-131)

The Union Jack also appeared on both the 1910–1928 and 1928–1994 [flags](/wiki/South_African_Flag#History "South African Flag") of South Africa. The [1910–1928 flag](/wiki/South_Africa_Red_Ensign "South Africa Red Ensign") was a [Red Ensign](/wiki/Red_Ensign "Red Ensign") with the [Union coat of arms](/wiki/Coat_of_arms_of_South_Africa_(1910%E2%80%932000) "Coat of arms of South Africa (1910–2000)") in the fly. The 1928–1994 flag, based on the [Prinsenvlag](/wiki/Prinsenvlag "Prinsenvlag") and commonly known as the *oranje-blanje-blou* (orange-white-blue), contained the Union Jack as part of a central motif at par with the flags of the two [Boer republics](/wiki/Boer_republics "Boer republics") of the [Orange Free State](/wiki/Orange_Free_State "Orange Free State") and [Transvaal](/wiki/South_African_Republic "South African Republic"). To keep any one of the three flags from having precedence, the Union Jack is spread horizontally from the Orange Free State flag towards the hoist; closest to the hoist, it is in the superior position but since it is reversed it does not precede the other flags. The present [flag of South Africa](/wiki/Flag_of_South_Africa "Flag of South Africa") features a nod to the Union Jack in its red, white and blue portion, the colours also harking to the [South African Republic](/wiki/South_African_Republic "South African Republic") and the [Dutch tricolour](/wiki/Flag_of_the_Netherlands "Flag of the Netherlands").[[131]](#cite_note-132)

The flag of the Municipal Council of [Shanghai International Settlement](/wiki/Shanghai_International_Settlement "Shanghai International Settlement") in 1869 contained multiple flags to symbolise the countries have participated in the creation and management of this enclave in the Chinese city of Shanghai. The Union Jack was contained as part of top left hand shield and close to the flags of the United States and [France](/wiki/Second_French_Empire "Second French Empire"), there was also contained the [flag of Prussia](/wiki/Flag_of_Prussia "Flag of Prussia") nearby, but it was removed around 1917.

The flag of the [Chilean](/wiki/Chile "Chile") city of [Coquimbo](/wiki/Coquimbo "Coquimbo") features the Union Jack, owing to its historical commercial links to Britain.

### Ensigns

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Union_Jack&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Ensigns")]

Further information: [British ensigns](/wiki/British_ensigns "British ensigns")

Unsourced material may be challenged and [removed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Burden_of_evidence "Wikipedia:Verifiability"). *(May 2011)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

The Union Flag can be found in the canton of several of the [ensigns](/wiki/Ensign_(flag) "Ensign (flag)") flown by vessels and aircraft of the United Kingdom and its overseas territories, and on land at locations associated with the organisation which the ensign represents, for example government offices, military establishments, etc. These are used in cases where it is illegal or inappropriate to fly the Union Flag, such as at sea from a ship other than a British warship. Normal practice for British ships is to fly the [White ensign](/wiki/White_ensign "White ensign") (Royal Navy), the [Red ensign](/wiki/Red_ensign "Red ensign") (Merchant and private boats) or the [Blue ensign](/wiki/Blue_ensign "Blue ensign") (government departments and public corporations). Similar ensigns are used by other countries (such as [New Zealand](/wiki/List_of_New_Zealand_flags#Ensigns "List of New Zealand flags") and [Australia](/wiki/List_of_Australian_flags#Military_and_naval_flags "List of Australian flags")) with the Union Flag in the canton. Other Commonwealth countries (such as [India](/wiki/Indian_Military_Flags "Indian Military Flags") and [Jamaica](/wiki/List_of_Jamaican_flags#Ensigns "List of Jamaican flags")) may follow similar ensign etiquette as the UK, replacing the Union Flag with their own national flag.
