[![Union Flag with thick white border comprising about half of the area of the flag.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/Civil_Jack_of_the_United_Kingdom.svg/250px-Civil_Jack_of_the_United_Kingdom.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Civil_Jack_of_the_United_Kingdom.svg)

A *Pilot Jack*, a flag design that has a Union Jack at its centre, with a white border around it

The flag in a white border occasionally seen on merchant ships was sometimes referred to as the **Pilot Jack**. It can be traced back to 1823 when it was created as a signal flag, but not intended as a civil jack. A book[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] issued to British [consuls](/wiki/Consul_(representative) "Consul (representative)") in 1855 states that the white bordered Union Flag is to be hoisted for a pilot. Although there was some ambiguity regarding the legality of it being flown for any other purpose on civilian vessels, its use as an ensign or jack was established well in advance of the 1864 Act that designated the [Red Ensign](/wiki/Red_Ensign "Red Ensign") for merchant shipping.

In 1970, the white-bordered Union Flag ceased to be the signal for a pilot, but references to it as national colours were not removed from the current Merchant Shipping Act and it was legally interpreted as a flag that could be flown on a merchant ship, as a jack if desired. This status was confirmed to an extent by the Merchant Shipping (Registration, etc.) Act 1993 and the consolidating Merchant Shipping Act 1995 which, in Section 4, Subsection 1, prohibits the use of any distinctive national colours or those used or resembling flags or pendants on Her Majesty's Ships, "except the [Red Ensign](/wiki/Red_Ensign "Red Ensign"), the Union flag (commonly known as the Union Jack) with a white border", and some other exceptions permitted elsewhere in the Acts. However, Section 2 regards the "British flag", and states that "The flag which every British ship is entitled to fly is the Red Ensign (without any defacement or modification) and, subject to (a warrant from Her Majesty or from the Secretary of State, or an Order of Council from her Majesty regarding a defaced [Red Ensign](/wiki/Red_Ensign "Red Ensign")), no other colours."[[132]](#cite_note-133) The Flag Institute listed the white bordered Union Flag as "Civil Jack".[[18]](#cite_note-british-flag-protocol-19)

[![Union Flag with thick blue border the thickness of one-fifth the breadth of the flag.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/Naval_Jack_of_Royal_Indian_Marine.svg/250px-Naval_Jack_of_Royal_Indian_Marine.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Naval_Jack_of_Royal_Indian_Marine.svg)

Naval Jack of [Royal Indian Marine](/wiki/Royal_Indian_Navy "Royal Indian Navy") (1884-1928), a flag design that has a Union Jack at its centre, with a blue border around it

From 1884 until 1928, the [Royal Indian Marine](/wiki/Royal_Indian_Marine "Royal Indian Marine") flew a modified Union Jack bordered with blue as its jack. From 11 November 1928 until its dissolution in 1947, it substituted the [Blue Ensign](/wiki/Blue_Ensign "Blue Ensign") defaced with the [Star of India emblem](/wiki/Flags_of_British_India#Emblem "Flags of British India") as jack and flew the British [White ensign](/wiki/White_ensign "White ensign") as ensign.[[133]](#cite_note-134)

An unofficial flag for the British Empire was created around 1910 due to a belief that the Union Jack itself no longer sufficiently represented dominions such as Canada, which were beginning to adopt their own unique symbols. The flag was similar in design to a White Ensign, which features the Union Jack in the canton. In the other quadrants were the coats of arms of Canada, Australia, and South Africa. The four stars forming [the Crux](/wiki/Crux "Crux") over Saint George's Cross represented New Zealand, and the Star of India was featured prominently in the middle. Civilians often flew Empire flags for patriotic events such as coronations, [Empire Day](/wiki/Commonwealth_Day "Commonwealth Day"), and the [British Empire Exhibition](/wiki/British_Empire_Exhibition "British Empire Exhibition").[[134]](#cite_note-:1-135) It is still flown on special occasions at the [Dangarsleigh War Memorial](/wiki/Dangarsleigh_War_Memorial "Dangarsleigh War Memorial").[[135]](#cite_note-136)

The [Hudson's Bay Company](/wiki/Hudson%27s_Bay_Company "Hudson's Bay Company") (HBC) and [East India Company](/wiki/East_India_Company "East India Company") were two of only a few non-government institutions using the Union Jack in part of their flags. HBC rival [North West Company](/wiki/North_West_Company "North West Company") had a similar flag to that of the HBC. The HBC Red Ensign was in use from 1801 to 1965 and was replaced with a corporate flag featuring the company's coat of arms. The East India Company, founded in 1600 and dissolved in 1874, used a red and white striped flag featuring, firstly, the flag of England, then flag of Great Britain and ultimately the flag of the United Kingdom in the canton.

The Union Jack is the third [quarter](/wiki/Quartering_(heraldry) "Quartering (heraldry)") of the 1939 [coat of arms of Alabama](/wiki/Coat_of_arms_of_Alabama "Coat of arms of Alabama"), which is used on the flag of the governor of Alabama, representing British sovereignty over the state prior to 1783. The version used is the modern flag, whereas the 1707 flag would have been used in colonial Alabama.

A pink Union Jack, with the blue triangles of the Union Jack changed to pink in reference to the [pink triangle](/wiki/Pink_triangle "Pink triangle") symbol, was created by a gay man, David Gwinnutt, to express his "pride in being gay and British."[[136]](#cite_note-137)[[137]](#cite_note-Pink-138)[[138]](#cite_note-139)

In the former International Settlement of [Kulangsu](/wiki/Kulangsu "Kulangsu") the Kulangsu Municipal Police had a badge contained multiple flags, including the Union Flag. The badge was incorporated into their police flag.[[139]](#cite_note-140)

## In popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Union_Jack&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: In popular culture")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/Geri_Halliwell_in_concert_cropped.jpg/250px-Geri_Halliwell_in_concert_cropped.jpg)](/wiki/File:Geri_Halliwell_in_concert_cropped.jpg)

[Geri Halliwell](/wiki/Geri_Halliwell "Geri Halliwell") of the [Spice Girls](/wiki/Spice_Girls "Spice Girls") wearing a remake of the Union Jack dress she wore at the [1997 Brit Awards](/wiki/1997_Brit_Awards "1997 Brit Awards")

The Union Jack remains one of the most instantly-recognisable flags in the world.[[140]](#cite_note-141) This is chiefly due not only to its unusual design, but the influence of [British](/wiki/Britishness "Britishness") [culture](/wiki/Culture_of_the_United_Kingdom "Culture of the United Kingdom") across the globe as a result of the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire"), and its resulting presence in several [Commonwealth](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations") nations' flags and heraldry.

While most of the former colonies of the British Empire have chosen to omit the Union Jack in their national flags, some countries such as [Australia](/wiki/Flag_of_Australia "Flag of Australia") and [New Zealand](/wiki/Flag_of_New_Zealand "Flag of New Zealand") have chosen to keep the Union Jack as a symbol of their British heritage, despite numerous attempts to change it.[[141]](#cite_note-142)[[142]](#cite_note-143)

The Union Flag has been a prominent symbol in the sphere of [fashion](/wiki/Fashion "Fashion") since the [British Invasion](/wiki/British_Invasion "British Invasion") movement of the 1960s, in a similar manner to the American [Stars and Stripes flag](/wiki/Stars_and_Stripes_flag "Stars and Stripes flag"), and came back into fashion in the mid-1990s '[Cool Britannia](/wiki/Cool_Britannia "Cool Britannia")' era, notably [Noel Gallagher](/wiki/Noel_Gallagher "Noel Gallagher")'s Union Jack [Epiphone Sheraton](/wiki/Epiphone_Sheraton "Epiphone Sheraton") II electric guitar, and [Geri Halliwell's dress](/wiki/Union_Jack_dress "Union Jack dress") at the [1997 Brit Awards](/wiki/1997_Brit_Awards "1997 Brit Awards").[[143]](#cite_note-144)[[144]](#cite_note-145) A notable increase in popularity was seen in [Cuba](/wiki/Cuba "Cuba") following the [2012 London Olympics](/wiki/2012_London_Olympics "2012 London Olympics"), with clothing, nail decoration, tattoos, and hairstyles in youths being observed featuring the pattern.[[145]](#cite_note-146) The flag became a common symbol in the underground UK rap scene in the 2020s.[[146]](#cite_note-147)[[147]](#cite_note-148)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/Flag_of_the_United_Kingdom_%28Watch_Dogs_Legion%29.svg/250px-Flag_of_the_United_Kingdom_%28Watch_Dogs_Legion%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Flag_of_the_United_Kingdom_(Watch_Dogs_Legion).svg)

The fictional Union flag design as appears in [Watch Dogs: Legion](/wiki/Watch_Dogs:_Legion "Watch Dogs: Legion")

A hypothetical Union flag design appears in the [Ubisoft](/wiki/Ubisoft "Ubisoft") published [action adventure game](/wiki/Action_adventure_game "Action adventure game") titled [Watch Dogs: Legion](/wiki/Watch_Dogs:_Legion "Watch Dogs: Legion"), where following [Scottish independence](/wiki/Scottish_independence "Scottish independence") from the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), the colour of the Union flag is altered to reflect the absence of the *Azure* (blue) field of the flag of Scotland, which is replaced by the *Sable* (black) field of the [Flag of Saint David](/wiki/Flag_of_Saint_David "Flag of Saint David"), patron saint of [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales"). The *Or* (gold) cross of [Saint David](/wiki/Saint_David "Saint David") forms a fimbriated border to the *Gules* (red) cross of [Saint George](/wiki/Saint_George "Saint George").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Subsumation in corporate branding

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Union_Jack&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Subsumation in corporate branding")]

Commonly the Union Flag is used on computer software and Internet pages as an [icon](/wiki/Computer_icon "Computer icon") representing a choice of the [English language](/wiki/English_language "English language") where a choice amongst multiple languages may be presented to the user, though the American flag is also sometimes used for this purpose. The flag has been embroidered on various [Reebok](/wiki/Reebok "Reebok") equipment as a mark of the brand's British origin,[[148]](#cite_note-149) and the Reebok Union Jack has been referred to as a brand icon.[[149]](#cite_note-upshaw-150) Many music artists have used the Union Jack, including [the Rolling Stones](/wiki/The_Rolling_Stones "The Rolling Stones"), [the Who](/wiki/The_Who "The Who"), [the Kinks](/wiki/The_Kinks "The Kinks"), [the Jam](/wiki/The_Jam "The Jam"), [Sex Pistols](/wiki/Sex_Pistols "Sex Pistols"), [the Stone Roses](/wiki/The_Stone_Roses "The Stone Roses"), [David Bowie](/wiki/David_Bowie "David Bowie"), [John Lennon](/wiki/John_Lennon "John Lennon"), [Paul McCartney](/wiki/Paul_McCartney "Paul McCartney"), [Freddie Mercury](/wiki/Freddie_Mercury "Freddie Mercury"), [Morrissey](/wiki/Morrissey "Morrissey"), [Oasis](/wiki/Oasis_(band) "Oasis (band)"), [Blur](/wiki/Blur_(band) "Blur (band)"), [Iron Maiden](/wiki/Iron_Maiden "Iron Maiden"), [Def Leppard](/wiki/Def_Leppard#Hysteria_(1984–1989) "Def Leppard"), and the [Spice Girls](/wiki/Spice_Girls#Cool_Britannia "Spice Girls").

[British Airways](/wiki/British_Airways "British Airways") painted a cropped Union Flag on their tail fins until 1984; from 1997 on, they started painting a stylised, fluttering Union Flag on tail fins, nicknamed *Chatham Dockyard Union Flag*. The coat of arms of British Airways also features the [escutcheon](/wiki/Escutcheon_(heraldry) "Escutcheon (heraldry)") cropped from the Union Flag.[[150]](#cite_note-151) British Airways used the coat of arms on their aircraft via the Landor livery between 1984 and 1997 and added back in 2011; they also used it as cap badge for pilots or in advertisements.[[151]](#cite_note-152)[[152]](#cite_note-153)[[153]](#cite_note-154)

The BMW Mini tail lights are shaped after the Union Jack.[[154]](#cite_note-155)
